star.story.role.wind_yaose:
He first 'saw' the wind when he was five, after losing his sight. His mother led him to the platform, and the air from the arriving train rushed against his face. He said, "The wind is grey, and it runs very fast." His mother cried, but he smiled.

Later, he often took the train alone, pressing his face against the window glass to feel the roaring currents outside. The conductor recognized the blind boy and would occasionally offer him a cup of warm water. He would thank them, listening to the sound of the water. Passengers passed by, their footsteps varying in weight; from their steps, he could tell who was happy and who was afraid.

That night, the train entered a long tunnel. As usual, he pressed his face to the window, but suddenly felt the wind change—no longer a gentle caress, but a forceful push, squeezing the entire train to one side. Before he could grab a handrail, the world turned upside down. A loud crash, screams, the screech of twisting metal, then an endless silence.

He floated up, light as a speck of dust. He saw his own body being carried away on a stretcher, his face peaceful, a faint smile on his lips. He wanted to shout "I'm here," but no one looked back. He passed through the train's carriage wall like passing through water, saw the terrified faces, saw those lying in the aisle, and saw a man crouching and trembling under a seat. The handle of a knife peeked out from the man's pocket, the tip stained with blood.

He recognized those footsteps—when that person boarded, they had passed by him, steps very light, as if afraid of stepping on something. He had heard the person whisper softly, "Excuse me," a voice low and gentle, like wind through a crack in a door. He liked that voice.

He floated next to the person, who was now looking around anxiously. Their gaze passed right through him, seeing nothing. He suddenly realized he could remain unseen forever. He tried to concentrate, and the surrounding cries for help suddenly grew quieter, as if someone had turned down the volume. The person paused, then quickly stood up and felt his way towards another carriage. The Wind Elf followed behind. Seeing someone give chase, he gently blew a puff of air, a gust of wind pushing them back.

He didn't know why he helped this person with the knife. Perhaps it was because of that voice, or because the person had instinctively reached out a hand, trying to steady a falling child amidst the chaos—even though they didn't manage to.

The train kept moving forward, the tunnel seemingly endless. And so he floated, hiding in the air of every carriage, 'seeing' everything with his wind senses. He knew that as long as that person could make it to the end, he could follow them out of this long night.

After all, the wind never cares about direction, only about flowing.
star.story.modifier.expedition:
The train cuts through the permafrost of the eternal night; the snowstorm outside scrapes against the windows like a knife. He leans against the carriage connector, wrapped in that faded expedition coat, his brim pulled low. In his pocket is a rust-covered badge—the only thing that can prove his identity, but in this world, identity has long lost its meaning.

The good people in the carriage are dwindling. He watched the elderly woman who gave up her seat for a child collapse in the aisle. He saw the young man who always played the harmonica late at night get dragged into the darkness. He couldn't save anyone. He was just a guide, someone who knew the map but couldn't wield a weapon.

Now, only he and one other survivor remain. A young girl, huddled and trembling under a seat, clutching a torn ticket stub. The overhead no longer announces stations, only a hissing static, like the breath of some creature.

He takes off his hat, his exhaled white breath blurring his vision. He remembers the words of the team leader before they set out: "The goal of the Expedition team is to arrive, not to fight." But at this moment, the destination no longer matters. What matters is whether they can live to see.

Just as he lowers his head, the badge in his pocket suddenly heats up. He pulls it out. The rusted iron badge is flaking away, revealing a dark red beneath: a red star, a sickle, an anchor chain. He freezes. This was passed down from his grandfather's grandfather—a military branch long vanished, a history buried under the permafrost.

The carriage lights go out completely, leaving only the pale glare of the emergency lights. His hand touches cold metal—a Derringer materializes at his waist, and a hand grenade hangs on the other side. His vision pierces through the carriage wall, through the snowstorm, and he sees the lurking shapes in the darkness, sees their positions, sees their paths of movement.

The girl looks at him in terror. His eyes have changed; they are now as cold and bright as the starry sky over a polar winter night.

"Hide," he whispers. The voice doesn't sound like his own. It sounds like countless throats that have roared across the icy plains, layered together.

He stands up, the hem of his coat sweeping across the snow-covered floor. The Derringer spins in his palm, its barrel pointing towards the end of the carriage. He pulls the trigger, the bullet firing into the darkness. He senses the bullet hit something, and the chamber instantly refills with a new round. The pin of the grenade clicks lightly between his fingers, he swings his arm and throws. The explosion's light illuminates the entire carriage, as well as the twisted shadows within.

The snowstorm surges in, and he stands between the firelight and the ice crystals, as if standing on some distant battlefield. There, the sea froze, the red flags flew, and the soldiers had no way back, only forward.

He is no longer an Expedition member. He is the Red Navy, the last one, and the first.

The train still moves forward; the destination may never come. But he grips his gun tightly and walks towards the darkness, each step leaving a deep imprint on the icy floor, like an inscription etched into the permafrost.
star.story.modifier.lovers:
The train crossed a long tunnel; the darkness outside the window was like coagulated river water. She leaned against her seat, her fingers unconsciously rubbing the window frame, when suddenly she felt a palpitation—not fear, but a strange pull, as if someone not far away was breathing at the same rhythm as her.

She looked up, through the dimly lit carriage, and met a pair of eyes. The person was three rows away, looking back at her with the same confusion in their gaze. Just for a moment, they both averted their eyes, but the invisible thread had already tied them together.

Later, she learned that he felt it too. Whenever her heart raced, his pulse would falter; whenever danger neared him, her palms would grow cold. They never spoke, yet they understood each other's situation better than anyone. The train swayed in the night. Passengers slowly showed their fangs; some fell in the aisles, others disappeared at the carriage junctions. They exchanged occasional glances across the crowd, then continued their separate struggles.

That night, she saw him surrounded by three people. Her heart clenched so tightly it ached terribly. She wanted to rush over, but her legs were like lead. Just then, one of the three suddenly clutched his throat and fell. The other two scattered in terror. He stood in the pool of blood, a knife in his hand, looking up at her. She wasn't afraid; she just nodded slightly.

The killing didn't stop. The good grew fewer, and the wicked devoured each other. They tacitly avoided each other but cleared the threats around each other. He killed his own comrades—the killers he once stood shoulder to shoulder with—because she was still there, because the only victory was when the two of them survived until the end.

Finally, the carriage fell silent. Only the hissing static remained on the overheads. All the seats were empty except for the two facing each other across the gap of three rows. He walked towards her, each step crunching on broken glass. She stood there, waiting for him.

When he finally stood before her, they reached out their hands at the same time. The moment their fingertips touched, the train gave a violent shake, and then all the lights went out. In the darkness, they heard a distant whistle, long and desolate, like an ancient blessing.

They didn't speak. They just sat down, side by side, waiting for the destination. Whether it was dawn, or an even deeper night.
star.story.role.loose_end:
The Two Minutes After I Die
One
What is my name?

On this Orient Express, no one has ever asked that question.

The ticket has a string of letters and numbers printed on it. The conductor only counts heads when checking the list. The dining car attendant only takes orders by room number. Even the man sitting across from me, with his round glasses, only nodded when he sat down, then lowered his head to read his newspaper.

"Compartment Nine," he said, almost to himself.

That was three days ago.

In three days, I've seen a lot of people come and go on this train. The woman in the camel-hair coat from First Class goes to the dining car for a Martini every evening. Three olives, no more, no less. There's a man in a flat cap who always hangs around the cargo carriage, never speaking to anyone. The door to the sick bay is always closed, but a few times when I passed by, I heard strange sounds coming from inside—not moaning, not conversation, but more like something dragging across the floor.

I didn't ask.

On this train, no one asks questions.

Just like I never told anyone that every night, I go to the back of the train to stand for a while. It's where the wind is strongest, and it can blow the things in my head away. I don't know what I'm thinking about, or rather, I don't know what I'm supposed to be thinking about. It's been three years now. That feeling has always stayed with me—like an ill-fitting piece of clothing worn so long, I've forgotten what 'fitting' ever felt like.

I remember a subway station in some city, a trash can at some alley entrance. I remember a piece of cardboard under a bridge that was always wet. But I don't remember how I got to those places, or who I was before that.

Sometimes I have a dream.

There are many people in the dream. They all have their heads down, walking forward in a line. I don't know where they're going, but I know I don't want to follow. I run. I run as hard as I can. I run until I can't breathe, until my legs are like lead, until—

I wake up.

Every time I wake up somewhere different.

This time, it was on a train heading east.

Two

The lights in the dining car that evening were as dim as aged brandy.

I sat by the window. Outside, the sky had already darkened, with occasional scattered lights flashing by, like memories I couldn't quite grasp. My black tea had already been refilled for the second time, the lemon slice curled at the bottom of the cup.

The man across from me, with the round glasses, was stirring his soup with a silver spoon. He moved very slowly, very evenly, as if he'd done it countless times. We'd met in the dining car almost every day at this time for three days, sitting at the same table, ordering similar things, eating, and leaving separately.

Today he had ordered soup.

"You look like something's on your mind," he said, not looking up.

I looked out the window: "Nothing."

"There is." His spoon paused for a second. "Your hand."

I looked down at my hand. It was trembling.

Strange. I hadn't noticed.

"Everyone carries something," he put his spoon down and wiped his mouth with a napkin. "On this train, the strange ones are those without anything."

I didn't respond.

Another light flashed by the window, this time a whole cluster of lights—a small station, a few houses, an empty platform. The train didn't stop, just passed it by.

There was a person on that platform, standing still, watching the train pass.

I blinked.

The person was gone.

Maybe there was never a person there.

Maybe there was.

My finger twitched again.

"What are you looking for?" the man across the table suddenly asked.

I looked up.

His eyes were hidden behind his glasses, impossible to read, but I could feel him watching me. Not a casual glance, but a deeper look, as if trying to see right through me.

"Nothing," I said.

"Everyone is looking for something." He lifted his bowl and took a sip. "Things, people, answers. Some people find it, some don't. Some people find it, only to realize it wasn't what they wanted."

He put the bowl down.

"What about you? Which kind are you?"

I thought for a moment.

"I don't even know what I'm looking for."

He laughed. The smile was brief, gone in an instant, like some habitual expression.

"Then you're the luckiest kind," he said. "Because you still have time to figure it out."

Outside, it was completely dark.

The train entered a tunnel.

The dining car lights flickered once.

I heard a sound.

A very light, thin sound, like something was approaching.

Then—

A gunshot.

The bullet went in through my left temple and came out the right, taking with it a few things I wasn't particularly attached to.

Like, my life.

As I fell, I saw the man with the round glasses stand up, pull a handkerchief from his pocket, and dab at the blood splattered on his cuff.

His expression was as calm as if he'd just squashed an ant.

I saw a letter embroidered on his handkerchief.

I couldn't tell which letter.

I closed my eyes.

Then, darkness.

Three

What does a person see after death?

When I was a child, the old people told me you see a river, with an old ferryman at the bank, and you have to pay to cross. The poor would wait a thousand years on the shore.

Others said you'd see your whole life flash before your eyes, like a movie, good and bad, everything forced on you again.

Still others said there was nothing, just like falling asleep, never to wake up.

They were all wrong.

The answer is: a train.

The same Orient Express.

But different from when I was alive. When alive, I could only see the carriages, the seats, the windows, the people. Now, I can see so much more.

I see that everyone's outline carries a color.

Green, red, orange, blue, purple—like an abstract painting splashed with fluorescent paint. Some people's colors are very pale, as if about to fade away. Some are so intense they're blinding.

I see the woman in the camel-hair coat. Her outline is red. Not a gentle red, but the color of blood, the color of fire, the color of the smile at the corner of her mouth when she looks down at my corpse.

Killer. She's a Killer.

I see the man with the round glasses. His outline is also red. But he's special—there's a yellow light in the middle of his red, as if marked by something.

I see the others in the dining car. Some green, some red, and a few with colors I've never seen before—something like purple mixed with blue, or perhaps without any color at all.

I see myself, too.

My corpse lies on the floor of the dining car. The lamb chops are still steaming, the tea is cold. Blood still seeps from the bullet wound, the pupils already scattered. Conductors are evacuating passengers. Some scream, some pretend to scream, some are as silent as statues.

A man in uniform runs over, crouches, and feels for my pulse.

"He's dead," he says.

Then he stands up and starts questioning: "Anyone see what happened?"

No one saw.

Or rather, no one said they saw.

The man with the round glasses is talking to a conductor, looking innocent and anxious, like a frightened ordinary passenger. The woman in the camel-hair coat has already retreated to the other end of the carriage, holding a glass of wine, chatting with an old man.

I see her smile.

I see that when she smiles, her eyes flick towards me.

Not at my corpse.

At me.

She can see me?

No. Impossible. Her gaze passes right through me, landing on something behind.

But at that moment, I feel it.

She's checking.

Checking if I'm really dead.

Then, I see a person.

She's wearing a grey trench coat, her hair tied up very tight, her face utterly expressionless. She walks from the other end of the carriage, doesn't step around my corpse, steps right over it.

She crouches down, dips a finger in my blood, and sniffs it.

Then she smiles.

That smile is different from the Killer's.

The Killer's smile was triumph, satisfaction of a task completed.

Her smile is anticipation.

Like she's saying: finally, it begins.

At that moment, I see her outline.

Not red, not green, not any color I can name.

It's an... absence.

Like something should be there, but it's been gouged out.

She stands up and walks towards the other end of the carriage. As she passes the Killer in the camel-hair coat, she pauses for a second.

The Killer doesn't look at her.

But she looks at the Killer.

That look, like she's looking at a dead person.

I stare at her back until she disappears at the end of the carriage.

Then, I see it.

A piece of paper.

Sliding out of my pocket, landing in the pool of blood.

When I was alive, I had never seen this piece of paper before.

When was it put there? Who put it? Why?

The paper is half soaked in blood, but the words are still legible. Not printed, handwritten. The handwriting is very sloppy, like the hand was trembling as they wrote.

Only one line:

"You were never one of them."

What does that mean?

I stare at the paper, try to pick it up. But my hand passes right through it, like passing through air.

I try to look for the woman in the grey trench coat again, but she's gone.

Then, I hear a voice.

Not coming from the train's speakers, nor from anyone's mouth.

Coming from inside my head.

"Two minutes."

The voice says, very calm, like stating a fact.

"You have two minutes to decide whether to come back."

Come back?

I look down at my corpse. The lamb chops are no longer steaming. The blood has coagulated. The pupils are completely scattered. Can this body still be used?

"Not this body," the voice says. "The other body. The one you've always carried."

I don't understand.

But I feel something awakening inside me.

Not this dead body of mine, but another—

One that I've always carried, but never noticed.

It's moving.

Crawling.

Pushing its way out.

Like a rusted knife being forcibly drawn.

It hurts.

It hurts so much I almost scream.

But the dead don't scream.

"Two minutes," the voice repeats. "The countdown has started."

Four

What can you do in two minutes?

When alive, two minutes are only enough to drink half a cup of tea, or walk the length of one carriage, or listen to the intro of a song.

But two minutes after death allow you to see everything.

I see the Killer—the woman in the camel-hair coat—talking into a walkie-talkie. Her voice is low, but I can hear it very clearly. The dead can hear everything very clearly.

"Target eliminated," she says. "Did you get the thing he was carrying?"

Static crackles from the walkie-talkie, then the man with the round glasses replies: "He had nothing on him."

"Impossible."

"Really, nothing. We searched. Not even a scrap of paper."

The Killer frowns, looking down at my corpse.

Then she sees the paper lying in the pool of blood.

She bends down to pick it up.

Her hand passes through the paper.

Exactly like my own hand did.

Her expression changes.

At that moment, I see her outline flicker—changing from red to... no color at all. Like a TV with a bad signal, full of static.

She straightens up and takes a step back.

"What's wrong?" the walkie-talkie asks.

She doesn't answer.

She just stares at that piece of paper, at those words soaked in blood.

I suddenly realize that she isn't unable to see the paper.

She can see it.

But she can't touch it.

Just like me.

"Of course she can see it," the voice says. "That paper was only meant for the dead to see."

"Only the dead can clearly see who they are."

I see the Killer turn and quickly walk towards the other end of the carriage. Her steps are fast, almost running. She passes the spot where the grey-trench-coated woman stood just moments ago and suddenly stops.

She looks down at the floor.

There is a puddle of water there.

Not water. Blood.

But not my blood.

Whose, then?

The Killer crouches down and dips her finger in the blood. The moment her finger touches it, the blood vanishes.

As if it had never existed.

She stands up and looks around.

The grey-trench-coated woman is gone.

The Killer stands there, not moving. Her outline starts flickering again, more violently this time, as if struggling to maintain something.

Then, she smiles.

Not a triumphant smile, not an anticipatory smile.

It's a smile of resignation.

Like she's saying: So that's it.

She puts away the walkie-talkie, straightens her coat, and continues walking to the end of the carriage. Her steps return to normal, her expression calm again, as if nothing happened.

But I saw.

The way she walks has changed.

Before, she walked with her toes touching the ground first, light as a cat.

Now, she walks with her heels hitting first, heavy as lead.

Like something is pressing down on her.

"She knows she's being watched," the voice says. "But she doesn't know by whom."

"She will know soon enough."

I look down at my hands again.

Not the hands of that corpse.

But my hands, in this form.

They are changing color.

From transparent to red.

Not the red of that Killer, but a deeper, more intense red, like fire burning from hell.

"Outlaw."

The voice says. This time, its tone is different. No longer a calm statement, but a ritual-like announcement.

"One who has returned from death."

"Your task is to take them all with you."

"Not a single one left."

I don't ask who 'them' means.

I know.

That Killer.

The man with the round glasses.

Everyone on this train whose hands are stained with blood.

Maybe even those whose hands aren't stained, but their hearts think about staining.

Maybe even everyone.

Five

Two minutes are up.

The voice says: "Now, you can choose."

"Stay a corpse, lie here forever."

"Or come back."

"But the you who comes back will no longer be the same person who drank tea and watched the scenery."

"The you who comes back will be an Outlaw."

"You will see everyone."

"And everyone will see you."

"But not the you of now."

"It will be another you."

"The you who has always been there."

I look down at myself.

Not the corpse lying on the ground, but this form I have now—no feet, no shadow, only a blurry outline.

No.

Not an outline.

A solid body.

I am becoming solid.

From transparent, to translucent, to—

Flesh and blood.

Bones.

Skin.

Clothes.

And the knife.

I don't know where it came from, but it's in my hand. Grip tight, as if I've held it for a lifetime.

A line of text is engraved on the blade, the same as the words on the paper in my pocket:

"You were never one of them."

I look up.

The dining car is still the dining car, the people are still the people. But it's different.

I see their outlines.

Red. Green. And other strange colors.

I also see something else.

Things I couldn't see before.

Behind each person, there is a shadow.

Not their own shadow, but someone else's.

Behind that Killer, there are three shadows. Three faint shadows, as if about to vanish.

Behind the man with the round glasses, there are five.

Behind that 'passenger' who always pretends to sleep in the corner, there are seven.

And where the grey-trench-coated woman stood—

There is one shadow.

A very dense shadow.

Dense almost to the point of becoming solid.

That shadow is looking at me.

I blink.

The shadow is gone.

Six

The overhead announcement sounds.

"Attention passengers, due to an unexpected incident, the train will make an unscheduled stop at the next station. All passengers please remain in your seats and await instructions from staff."

People in the dining car start to stir. Some stand up to ask what's happening, some shrink in their seats too afraid to move, some take out their phones to call—no signal.

The Killer stands at the other end of the carriage, facing away from me.

She hasn't seen me yet.

Or rather, she hasn't recognized me.

I take a step forward.

My foot hits the floor, making a sound.

Not the light sound of a phantom, not the sound of a dead person.

A solid, hefty sound of the living.

The Killer turns.

She sees me.

In that moment, her pupils contract.

Not because she recognizes who I am.

But because—

In her eyes, I am a complete stranger.

A person who never appeared before.

A person who appeared out of thin air.

"Who are you?" she asks.

I don't answer.

I keep walking forward.

She takes a step back.

"Stop," she says. Her hand goes into her pocket.

I know what's in there.

A gun. Same model as the one that killed me.

I don't stop.

She pulls out the gun.

Aims it at me.

"Last chance," she says. "Who are you?"

I finally speak.

"You don't remember me?"

Her finger is on the trigger, but she doesn't pull it.

She hesitates.

She's thinking: Who is this person? Why haven't I seen him before? Why does he look so familiar?

I smile.

"You seemed pretty happy when you shot me just now."

Her pupils contract again.

This time, she recognizes me.

Not by my face—my face is different from before.

By my eyes.

She's seen that look before.

On the floor of the dining car, in the pool of blood, in the eyes of that dead person whose pupils had already scattered.

"Impossible," she says. Her voice trembles.

"Nothing's impossible."

I keep walking forward.

She fires.

The bullet goes through my chest.

I don't stop.

She fires again.

Again.

Again.

Six bullets, all empty.

I stand in front of her, looking down.

Her face is completely white. Her hand is trembling. The gun falls to the floor.

"Who... who are you?"

I think for a moment.

"The 'me' from before... I don't know who that was either," I say. "But the 'me' now—"

I raise the knife.

The blade reflects her face.

On that face is an expression she's never seen before when pulling the trigger on someone else.

Fear.

"My name is Outlaw."

The knife falls.

Seven

Two minutes later, three people were dead on the train.

The Killer, the man with the round glasses, and the 'passenger' who always pretended to sleep in the corner. There was a gun in his pocket, the same model as the Killer's.

I stand among the three corpses. The knife in my hand is still dripping blood.

Someone screams and runs off. Someone hides in a compartment and locks the door. Someone raises a gun and points it at me.

I ignore them.

I look down at the Killer.

She lies on the floor, eyes still open, pupils scattered.

Exactly the same as when she shot me.

I crouch down and pull the walkie-talkie from her pocket.

Press the talk button.

"Hello."

Static hisses from the walkie-talkie. Then a woman's voice.

The grey-trench-coated woman's voice.

"You're back."

Not a question. A statement.

"You know me," I say.

"I've known you for a long time."

"Who are you?"

She pauses for a moment.

Then she says:

"Someone just like you."

The walkie-talkie hisses with interference, then cuts out.

I stand up and put the walkie-talkie away.

The night outside the window is as thick as ink.

The train stops.

The overhead announcement sounds again.

"Attention passengers, the unscheduled stop has concluded. The train will now continue forward. Please return to your seats."

Continue forward?

I look out the window.

On the platform stands a woman.

Grey trench coat. Hair tied up very tight. An expressionless face.

She's looking at me.

I raise the knife and look at my reflection in the window glass.

The person in the glass, I don't recognize.

But he has eyes that I recognize.

Those eyes have seen death.

Now, death sees him.

The woman outside turns, walks into the night, and disappears.

The train slowly starts moving.

I put away the knife and walk towards the next carriage.

There are many people still on this train.

And I have plenty of time.

After all, I've already died once.

Dying again wouldn't be a big deal.

[The End]

# Chapter 1: The Dusty Summons

Outside Gensokyo, in the blurry crack between the present world and the other shore, there exists an unremarkable curio shop. The shop is filled with things ordinary people cannot look upon: masks that devour their owners' dreams, man-eating flowers whose seeds have yet to sprout, and a dagger said to always drip blood yet has no visible blade. These cursed objects sleep silently in the dust, and guarding them is a wooden-faced old man wearing round spectacles.

In the darkest corner of the shop, on a dust-laden shelf, sits a young girl with her hair in two buns, looking no more than seven or eight years old. She swings her legs, her empty gaze fixed on the closed shop door.

She is a Tsukumogami.

In the world of Touhou Project, Tsukumogami are spirits that form from tools after years of accumulated belief or abandonment. The young girl's true form is that cursed bell from folklore, said to summon calamity. Legend has it that a whole family vanished because of her; neighbors whispered, "That family smuggled contraband, and his wife and children looked eerie too. They just disappeared into thin air one night." The bell changed hands several times before finally being sold here as a dangerous item.

"So bored..." The girl rested her chin on her hand and let out a long yawn. Her voice didn't echo in the space crammed with old things. "No one wants to buy me? No one comes to ring me? Stuck in this corner, no one will ever see me."

She glanced at the old man behind the counter. The shopkeeper, as he was called, was slowly wiping a tea bowl, as if those cursed objects were just ordinary clay pots.

"Who is this shopkeeper, really?" the girl wondered. Every previous owner, no matter how carefully they enshrined her, eventually met some unspeakable misfortune. But this old man piled them, these 'troublesome goods', around casually, as if he wanted them to sleep forever. "Is he really planning to let these cursed things sleep until the end of time?"

The girl looked at her own translucent fingertips. She was too young, unable to do anything besides 'summon calamity', without even a name of her own. In the world of Tsukumogami, someone like her, without a name, was no better than a lonely ghost.

Just as she was about to doze off, the bell on the shop door rang.

"Welcome." The shopkeeper greeted slowly.

The man who entered wore sunglasses and looked like a butler. He looked nervous, glancing around as soon as he entered: "Got anything good? These all look like trouble, you're just trying to get rid of them, aren't you?"

The bell girl watched listlessly. Another ordinary customer. Could they buy me, maybe...

But the next second, her face changed.

An extremely heavy, cold, oppressive 'aura' instantly enveloped the entire shop. It smelled thick like blood, yet carried the unique arrogance of someone at the top.

"This presence... could it be..."

Tap. Tap.

The crisp sound of leather shoes on the floor. Following the sound, a young girl in a gorgeous dress, with golden curls, stepped into the shop. Although her appearance was youthful, those sharp red eyes held a dignity far beyond her years, followed by a silent maid.

"So red..." The bell girl hid in the corner, her body trembling involuntarily. That was the color of blood, the color of a true strong one.

—A vampire. And one at the very pinnacle of vampires.

In the legends of Gensokyo, by the shores of Misty Lake stood a Western-style mansion marked by red window frames, the Scarlet Devil Mansion. The vampire who lived there, Remilia Scarlet, was a young lady with five hundred years of life, who once shrouded all of Gensokyo in red mist to block out the sun, causing the famous Scarlet Mist Incident.

Now, this young lady was looking around the cramped shop with interest, a playful smile on her lips: "Hmph, I see. It's cramped, but it's quite a nice shop. Might there be something interesting here?"

The old shopkeeper behind the counter pushed up his glasses, his tone still flat: "Oh my, a rare guest. A Western young lady, welcome."

# Chapter 2: The Scarlet Gaze

The vampire young lady—Remilia Scarlet—let her gaze sweep over the cursed masks and strange jars as if admiring roadside pebbles. The magician accompanying her, Patchouli Knowledge, had an indifferent look, even holding a thick grimoire in her hands, as if ready to record anything interesting.

"Ah, a place like this, hiding so many things full of 'resentment', truly delights one's heart and mind." Remilia chuckled lightly, then fixed her gaze on the shopkeeper. "You over there, you know what I want, don't you?"

The shopkeeper leisurely wiped the teacup in his hand, then slowly spoke: "For the young lady of the Scarlet Devil Mansion herself to appear in person, common wares naturally wouldn't catch your eye. You're looking for that item that can 'change fate'?"

The bell girl hiding in the corner of the shelf had already held her breath. She could feel the negative energy in the air, invisible to the naked eye, surging crazily due to Remilia's arrival. That was the pressure unique to an apex predator.

"How terrifying... this is a top-class youkai?" The bell girl hugged her knees tighter. Her own ability to 'summon misfortune' was nothing but a firefly before the bright moon compared to the vampire's aura of controlling fate.

What frightened her more was that the vampire young lady's gaze seemed to be deliberately drifting towards her corner.

"Hmph, things like fate, I've already grasped them long ago." Remilia raised her chin, her tone unyieldingly arrogant. "I heard you have an item here from a family that 'fled into the night'. That sound, it's interesting."

The shopkeeper paused his hand on the teacup, finally looked up, a deep light flashing behind his glasses: "You mean that bell? That's not a toy that brings pleasure. What it summons is outright calamity."

"Calamity? To me, that's just seasoning for life." Remilia took a step forward, the oppressive force like a mountain increasing again. "Bring it out. Let me see if it's worthy of entering my collection."

In the invisible realm, the bell girl saw the dark energy surrounding her being forcibly drawn by the vampire's power. Originally just an abandoned, cursed Tsukumogami, at this moment she felt like she was being swept into a giant vortex.

"A-am I going to be found out?"

She instinctively wanted to run, but her true form was the bell itself; there was nowhere to run.

The shopkeeper let out a faint sigh and took a red-clothed long case from under the counter. As the lid slowly opened, the delicate yet eerie calling bell revealed its true form.

The moment the bell appeared, the lamps in the curio shop flickered violently.

"Heh heh, this is the sound?" Remilia extended a slender finger, gently tapping towards the bell. But her finger hovered above the bell; she didn't press down immediately. Her crimson eyes looked past the physical object, staring at a point in the void—the spot where the bell girl was hiding.

"I see, it's already developed a 'consciousness', has it?" Remilia's lips curled, revealing a small fang.

The bell girl nearly fell off the shelf from fright. She had thought she was hiding well, but under the vampire's gaze that could see through fate, she felt as exposed as if standing naked under the blazing sun.

"Oh my, found out." The shopkeeper's tone was flat, as if he had expected it. "This child is timid, not used to meeting important people. Although burdened with the destiny of 'summoning calamity', she's just a little brat who likes to complain."

"I don't dislike that sense of destiny." Remilia withdrew her hand and turned to Patchouli. "Patchy, what do you think? Wouldn't life at the Scarlet Devil Mansion be a bit more interesting if we had this kind of 'misfortune'?"

The silent Patchouli turned a page in her thick book and replied blandly: "If you want the fairy maids to break a hundred teacups a day, or have Sakuya's knives inexplicably slip out of her hand, I don't mind. But only if this child can withstand the pressure of the Scarlet Devil Mansion."

The bell girl listened, trembling: These people... they're treating my terrifying curse as some kind of entertainment?

"So, name your price." Remilia looked at the shopkeeper.

The shopkeeper pushed up his glasses, his gaze lingering on the curled-up Tsukumogami for a second, then slowly said: "This item is not for sale. I just need a small favor from the young lady—this child has accumulated too much 'dust' (resentment) here. If she stays in the shop much longer, she might corrupt the other honest antiques. Please take her away and give this 'misfortune' an outlet."

It sounded like he was getting rid of a nuisance, but the bell girl was stunned. She understood. The shopkeeper saw that her power was out of control and wanted to find her a home where something could truly contain her and stop her from self-destructing.

"Fine, I'll find her a 'good job'." Remilia hesitated no longer and pressed the bell firmly with her fingertip.

"Ding——!!!"

A sound so crisp it was nearly piercing exploded in the shop. In that instant, black energy of misfortune surged out like a tide, but when it touched the red demonic aura around Remilia, it was roughly repelled, compressed, and eventually became docile.

"Alright, decided." Remilia grabbed the bell, then reached out and pinched the dazed bell girl's little face in the void. "From today, you are the 'welcome bell' of the Scarlet Devil Mansion. Only guests I allow, you call them 'in'. Those I don't like, you'll summon whatever disaster you want onto their heads!"

And so the bell girl was taken away from that dim curio shop. She looked back and saw the old shopkeeper with his back to the door, leisurely wiping the same teacup, as if nothing had ever happened.

# Chapter 3: New Life at the Scarlet Devil Mansion

The tide of memory recedes, the scene returns to the present.

Remilia leaned back, propping her chin on her hand with a bored expression, sitting in that wide European-style high-backed chair. Her slender fingertips lightly played with the bell that now belonged to her. It was her 'trophy' from that strange curio shop.

"Hey, still daydreaming?" Remilia stared at the bell girl's gradually appearing figure in the void, a mocking smile on her lips. "Seventeen years in that dusty old shop, and you still haven't seen enough of the outside world?"

The bell girl snapped back to reality. She tugged at her clothes awkwardly, looking at the lavishly decorated Western-style mansion hall before her.

This was the Scarlet Devil Mansion—a Western-style demon mansion situated by the Misty Lake in Gensokyo. Unlike the outside world, Gensokyo is a hidden land sealed off by the Hakurei Barrier, a secret realm inhabited by more non-human entities like youkai and gods than humans. And this Scarlet Devil Mansion was the residence of the vampire Remilia Scarlet.

There was no musty smell, no lifeless old junk inside. Instead, there were flickering red candles, the scent of precious black tea, and this extremely powerful, suffocating vampire young lady in front of her. Through the stained glass window, she could see the perpetual mist over the lake outside, and the occasional figures of fairy maids darting past.

"I was... just thinking about the shopkeeper..." the girl muttered, her voice still carrying a trace of reluctance for the past.

"That bald geezer doesn't need your concern." Remilia shifted her posture, her expression growing proud. "From the moment I pressed you, the old 'calamity-summoning' monster died. Now, you are my personal property, Remilia Scarlet's."

She stood up, her cape drawing an elegant arc through the air.

"Sakuya should have prepared your room. Although you're just a nameless little youkai, since you've entered the Scarlet Devil Mansion, show some spirit. Starting tomorrow, you'll guard the gate. And remember, those I don't like, you make sure they experience what true 'misfortune' is."

Remilia walked to the door, then stopped and added: "Oh, and Patchouli said she wants to study your 'calling' ability, so she might take you to the big library. It's the most extensive collection in Gensokyo, don't let those grimoires scare you."

The bell girl watched Remilia's retreating back, then looked down at her own hands. The loneliness of being cursed by fate and rejected by the world seemed to have found a strange kind of peace within this demonic, red-walled mansion.

She took a deep breath and, imitating the members of the Scarlet Devil Mansion, clumsily bowed.

"Yes, my lady."

The moment her voice faded, a commotion came from outside the door. A green-haired fairy maid ran panicking down the corridor, the tea tray in her hands tilted, on the verge of crashing to the floor. But the next second, time seemed to freeze for a moment, and the maid with silver-short hair, Head Maid Sakuya Izayoi, appeared next to the fairy out of nowhere, calmly catching all the falling tea sets.

"This is the daily life of the Scarlet Devil Mansion." Sakuya glanced blandly at the bell girl. "Welcome to the true 'Land of Misfortune'."

The bell girl was stunned, then couldn't help but laugh.

So, this was what they called 'home'?

# Epilogue

Several days later, at the main gate of the Scarlet Devil Mansion.

A young girl in a brand-new dress sat beside the gate bell, carefully observing every visitor. When a lost fairy tried to enter, she gently shook her body, and the bell gave a faint ring.

The next second, the fairy tripped over its own scattered magical starlight and fell headfirst into the fountain.

"Sorry, my lady says you're not welcome," the girl whispered.

In the distance, Remilia, holding her black tea and watching the scene through the window lattice, smiled with satisfaction.

"What a useful bell, indeed."

From then on, a small new existence was added to the legends of Gensokyo—the gate bell of the Scarlet Devil Mansion, ringing crisply for guests its mistress welcomed, but bringing true 'misfortune' to unwelcome intruders.

And she herself, finally found her own place in this fantasy land where youkai and humans coexist.
star.story.role.wayfarer:
The lights at the carriage connector flickered. He didn't look up, just fidgeted with the knife in his hand. The knife was fake, plastic, not even sharp. Just like the gun in his pocket, also with no bullets. They were his identity papers in this world—a passenger who shouldn't exist.

The speakers played a lazy jazz tune, the train moving smoothly through the night. Lights outside occasionally flickered across his face, a face so ordinary it would disappear in a crowd. No one noticed he never had his ticket checked, no one noticed his berth number was blank.

He stood up and walked slowly down the aisle. There weren't many people. Some were reading, some were in a daze. A woman was sleeping with her head tilted. He paused beside her—her face was grey-blue, her lips purple. She had stopped breathing long ago.

No one around noticed.

He crouched beside her, like tying his shoe, or just tired. The death was recent, the body not yet stiff. He leaned closer, smelled a certain faint odor, saw a very subtle powder under her fingernails. The killer used poison, and was experienced, not a first-timer. Fragments of information floated into his mind, not his own; things the corpse 'remembered'.

His hand paused on the knife handle.

Then he gripped it tighter. The plastic knife suddenly felt heavier, a cold glint flashing across the blade. At the same time, something new appeared in his pocket—a key, a letter. A gift from the corpse, or a bargain.

He stood up and continued walking.

The target was in the third carriage, a middle-aged man in a refined coat, reading a newspaper. As he passed by, the knife was already pressed against the man's ribs. The man started, tried to yell, but the knife was faster. This time, truly fast.

Blood soaked into the shirt, exactly the same way the woman had died.

Someone screamed. Someone stood up. But the refined-looking man had already slumped to the floor, newspaper scattered. No one noticed that the killer stood just a few steps away, his body turning transparent, like a flickering signal.

When he opened his eyes again, he was back beside the woman. Her head was still tilted, her face still grey-blue. He looked at her, then leaned back in his seat and closed his eyes.

The chaos around him continued. Someone ran for the conductor, someone tried first aid, someone just stared blankly. And in the middle of it all, his breathing gradually steadied, as if he could finally rest.

The night outside the window remained the same. The train still moved on.
star.story.role.maid_sakuya:
# Silver Blade and Pocket Watch

The Scarlet Devil Mansion by the Misty Lake stands in eternal twilight. The clock tower's hands are about to reach five.

Sakuya opened her eyes. She didn't have the habit of lazing in bed, nor did she need an alarm—the pocket watch hung by her bedside; one glance told her the time. Five o'clock on the dot, earlier than anyone else in the mansion, including the useless fairy maids.

She washed up, changed her clothes, tightened her hair ribbon. The silver-haired girl in the mirror had cold eyes. In a flash of her fingers, over a dozen throwing knives were hidden in the folds of her skirt. It was a habit, not a precaution—in this mansion, she didn't need to be on guard against anyone. Or rather, she hadn't needed to for a very long time.

A very long time.

The word was subtle to her. She claimed to be in her teens, yet carried herself like she'd lived for centuries. Some said she was from the outside world, some said she was a vampire hunter, others said she wasn't human at all. Sakuya never explained. When Remilia gave her this name, it seemed to also give her a past that didn't need questioning.

At five-fifteen, she appeared in the kitchen.

Time stopped.

All of Gensokyo froze in that instant: the fairy flying past the window stopped mid-air, the flames on the stove froze into orange sculptures, the water droplet from the tap hung three inches above the sink. Sakuya walked through the frozen world and began preparing breakfast.

She liked it this way. Not because of efficiency—though it certainly was efficient—but because of the peace of solitude. In the stopped time, no one watched her, no one needed a response from her; she could just be herself. As for who 'herself' was, she wasn't in a hurry to figure it out.

Remilia's breakfast needed to be warm, not too hot. Lady Patchouli's tea had to steep for exactly three minutes. Flandre's dessert needed to be hidden under a small plate, or she'd think it was a prank. And the gate guard, Hong Meiling—Sakuya paused, leaving an extra onigiri for the Chinese youkai. She'd fallen asleep at the gate again last night, but it was cold today; she should have a little more.

Time resumed.

The water droplet fell into the sink, the flames continued licking the bottom of the pan, the fairy maids shrieked as they flew past the window. Sakuya picked up the tray and walked towards the young lady's room.

"Sakuya, today's tea isn't sweet enough."

"Yes, my lady. I'll add sugar."

"Sakuya, I want to go play by the lake."

"Wait for the mist to clear; it's too cold outside right now."

"Sakuya—"

"I'm here."

Remilia leaned back against her pillow, her crimson eyes fixed on her for a moment, then suddenly smiled: "Your expression is softer today than yesterday."

Sakuya bowed slightly: "Because the weather is nice today."

"Liar." Remilia picked up her tea. "Oh well. You're always like this."

The morning cleaning lasted three hours. Even with the fairy maids' help, they couldn't even clean their own wings properly. Sakuya was used to it. She moved through the endless corridors of the Scarlet Devil Mansion, dusting windowsills, straightening bookshelves, changing water in vases. Every time she passed a window, she instinctively glanced outside.

The surface of Misty Lake was calm. Further off was the direction of the Hakurei Shrine.

Parties were often held there. She would attend, bringing homemade dishes, drinking sake with those humans who were once wary of her. Reimu never asked why she served a vampire, Marisa never asked why she could stop time. They just ate her cooking and said, "It's delicious."

That was enough.

Sakuya remembered that she couldn't really eat very hot food. Cat tongue, her only weakness. She'd tried to change it, couldn't. Maybe it was from some past experience, maybe just her constitution. She didn't dwell.

At two in the afternoon, she went out for supplies.

The human village wasn't as wary of her as before. The vegetable-selling granny would throw in an extra scallion, saying it was "for the young lady of the Scarlet Devil Mansion." Sakuya thanked her, paid, never took advantage. Passing by the Hieda house, she paused—Akyuu was probably writing again. About her background, about why she was here. She'd read all those guesses: vampire hunter, artificial human, lunar person, weirdo.

The most popular was the vampire hunter theory. That she'd hunted demons with silver knives, tracked them to the Scarlet Devil Mansion, been defeated, changed her name, and become a maid.

Sakuya touched the throwing knife at her waist. Silver, true. But was it used to hunt demons? Or just because she liked the luster of silver?

She couldn't quite remember.

Or perhaps she didn't want to.

Returning to the Scarlet Devil Mansion in the evening, Remilia was sitting on the balcony watching the sunset. Vampires didn't like the sun, but sunsets were acceptable. Sakuya walked over and stood behind her.

"Sakuya."

"Yes."

"Will you ever leave?"

Sakuya didn't answer right away. Remilia asked this question every so often, like a confirmation, or an insecurity. She looked at the young lady's back—someone others saw as powerful, willful, always above, now curled up in the chair, looking small as a child who truly needed care.

"No," Sakuya said.

"Why?"

"Because leaving a job with no pay feels too much of a loss."

Remilia turned back to glare at her. Sakuya's face was expressionless.

Then they both laughed.

Night fell. Sakuya made a final check of the mansion's doors and windows. The fairy maids were already asleep, Hong Meiling had finally woken up and was—earnestly daydreaming at the gate. The lights in Patchouli's library were still on, probably staying up late to read again. Flandre's room was quiet; no smashing today.

She returned to her own room, took off the pocket watch, and placed it by her pillow.

The moonlight outside was very bright. It was the sixteenth night, the first night after the full moon. That's what her name meant. Remilia had given it; whether casually or deliberately, she didn't know.

Sakuya lay down and closed her eyes.

Tomorrow's schedule was already set: cleaning, cooking, attending the young lady, supplies, dealing with incidents, preparing food for parties. She would steal a few moments of peace in the cracks of stopped time, frown at Hong Meiling's laziness, accept Remilia's capricious demands and carry them out expressionlessly.

No one knew her past.

Sometimes she wasn't even sure she knew it herself.

But when she lifted that freshly brewed cup of tea, tentatively took a sip, and frowned at the burn—in that moment, she felt very, very real.

Outside the window, the clock tower's hands passed twelve.

The head maid of the Scarlet Devil Mansion breathed evenly, asleep, dreaming of nothing.

The pocket watch ticked quietly by her pillow, second by second.
star.settings.role.maid_sakuya:
Maid of the Scarlet Devil Mansion
Sakuya Izayoi

Ability: Manipulation of time

She is the Stage 5 boss. As a maid, she has lived as a human for about 10 to 20 years.
Because of her ability, she was shunned by humans. She has already given up on getting along with ordinary people.
She has no desire for fame or ambition to dominate, feeling that having enough to eat is sufficient, which is why she works as a maid in the Scarlet Devil Mansion.

At the Scarlet Devil Mansion, she works as a cleaner and head maid, always utilizing her ability to stop time to get work done.
(It's said that dust doesn't fly when cleaning in stopped time)

Because the mistress of the house is a bit difficult, she is technically a maid, but actually more like a nanny, managing the household.

Spell Card attacks: 9 types.

As the head maid of the Scarlet Devil Mansion, Sakuya Izayoi spends most of her time inside, responsible for cleaning, assigning tasks to other maids, preparing daily meals, and attending to Lady Remilia. Occasionally, she also goes out to purchase daily necessities.

Despite the heavy workload, dealing with Remilia's various capricious demands, and having no salary, Sakuya never shows any dissatisfaction or negativity towards her job. Sometimes she even proactively accompanies the young lady in her leisure time. With her exceptional personal abilities and her power to manipulate time, she always perfectly completes all tasks.

As for why Sakuya works at the Scarlet Devil Mansion and what kind of person she was in the past, it remains a mystery. One widely circulated theory suggests she might have originally been a vampire hunter from the outside world, or another world, who came to the Scarlet Devil Mansion hunting a target but was defeated herself. Other guesses include artificial human, zombie, lunar person, or simply an oddball. However, the most influential is still the vampire hunter theory.

Besides her work at the Scarlet Devil Mansion, Sakuya also frequently attends parties at the Hakurei Shrine and is often responsible for preparing food for them. If it's to fulfill a request from Remilia or Patchouli, or if she senses signs of an incident, she will also travel around Gensokyo for that purpose.

Incidentally, she has a small habit—she's not good at eating hot food.
star.story.modifier.refugee:
In such a world, there is a group called 'Refugees'. They carry the weight of despair and the limits of survival. Once ordinary people, they lost their homes and their sense of belonging due to war, disaster, or some irresistible force.

When resources dry up and danger lurks around every corner, the instinct to survive begins to override everything else. You see them gradually change—they are no longer the gentle neighbor or kind stranger. After death, they become Outlaws. Morality, rules, past convictions, all peel away layer by layer, leaving only one thought: Survive.

What's even more unsettling is that such people often end up at the same conclusion—taking everyone else out. Not for revenge, not for redemption, but simply because they have already placed themselves on the opposite side of everyone else. They take out enemies, maybe comrades, and perhaps even the last remaining shred of hope.

This may not have been their intention, but it is the final note that fate writes for them in the face of absolute despair.
star.story.modifier.split_personality:
One body carries two distinctly different souls. They were born from the Harpy Train's emergency derailment. On the brink of death, some mysterious force forcefully stitched two independent consciousnesses into the same vessel. Usually, they take turns controlling the body's functions, a forced switch occurring about every minute. However, they can actively call for alternation through a resonance deep within their consciousness. The exterior always reflects the host personality's preference, while the secondary personality is confined to the dark room of consciousness, only able to glimpse the world through their host's eyes and hear all sounds.

Death is a cruel game for them. When the body's vital signs cease, both souls are pulled into a pure white spiritual space, facing the final choice: deceive the other, sacrificing their partner to survive alone; or sacrifice themselves, granting the other a chance at life. If they doubt each other and both choose Deceive, both soul and body will be completely annihilated. If one chooses Deceive while the other selflessly chooses Sacrifice, the betrayer returns to the world of the living with a burden of guilt, while the one who sacrificed is permanently dissolved. Only when both souls demonstrate pure sacrificial spirit and both choose Sacrifice does a miracle occur—both can temporarily return to the living world, but the cost is a mere sixty seconds of shared time, and they must carry all the bonds (items) they had at the time of death.

However, there is one extreme situation where even this twisted miracle of co-existence cannot reach: when the train encounters a horrifying event with completely chaotic rules known as the 'Refugee Moment', any death will be an irreversible finality. No game, no choice, only shared, eternal silence.
star.story.role.civilian:
You are simply the most ordinary passenger on this bizarre train. No extraordinary origins, no hidden powers, no mystical mission weighing on your back.
Like everyone else, you bought a ticket and boarded, expecting an ordinary journey, only to find yourself swept into a survival game beyond imagination. Your only weapons are a clear mind, the instinct to survive, and perhaps a faint glimmer of unextinguished courage. In these perilous carriages, your only goal is to try your best to survive this journey with no end in sight. Every decision, every observation, could mean life or death.
Your ordinariness, in this moment, has become your greatest trait, and your heaviest burden.
star.story.role.killer:
On the Harpy Train, you are not an accidental passenger. Your ticket itself is a 'job assignment' from some secret organization.
You are a professional killer, clean and efficient, your mind as cold as ice. Boarding this train is just another mission in your long career. Your goal is clear and merciless: eliminate all targets defined as 'Civilians'.
For this, you have access to a micro-terminal connecting to the organization's logistics—the 'Basic Killer Shop'. Though the supplies are humble—only the most fundamental tools like daggers, poison, and wire—for a true professional, that is more than enough.
You are not playing a game; you are just doing your job. Every corner of the carriage is a potential hunting ground, every brush of a shoulder could hide deadly intent. Trust is an unaffordable luxury. Your world contains only targets and the means to eliminate them. As the train races along unknown tracks, your hunt quietly begins.
star.story.role.vigilante:
He was once the embodiment of order, now just a lone wolf wandering the rails.
After the incident, he boarded this train with his only service pistol. The last bullet in the chamber is both his weapon and his sentence.
In the shadows of the carriage, he learned to trade, exchanging gathered resources for a pair of cold handcuffs, the only remaining symbol of the rules he once upheld.
His creed is etched into his bones: never point his gun at an innocent civilian. Any accidental kill will immediately trigger a backlash from the justice law deep within his heart, ending his own life.
The handcuffs are his non-lethal tool, a way to temporarily restrain a target, stripping them of the ability to fight or interact, pressing pause on the chaos.
But the restraint is not eternal. The imprisoned, by concentrating their will and strength and struggling continuously, can find a glimmer of hope to escape.
On this lawless train, he enforces a twisted kind of justice, walking the razor's edge between lawman and executioner.
star.story.role.discovery_civilian: "Thank you for riding the Harpy Express. All tourists, please check your carry-on luggage for anything left behind. If you find anything missing, please look for it at the Lost & Found on the platform." Boarding this train headed for the snowy country, everything seemed so peaceful. Seems tonight will be a safe night.
star.story.role.elf:
I once was a guardian of the forests. The twang of my bowstring was my dialogue with the world.

I cannot trust those metal creations that spew fire and smoke. Only this longbow, which accompanied me here, is my true extension.

Moving through the shadows of the train, I learned to utilize the resources here, modifying my longbow into a more deadly crossbow, and tempering two special types of arrows.

One is extracted from potent poison, lethal on contact, meant only to end the deepest darkness.

The other carries a faint light, capable of attaching to any surface. Its radiance can pierce through fog, revealing the outlines of those in hiding, or tracing the signs of life within an area.

I swore an oath: arrowheads should only point towards the true shadows.

Because I know that if an arrow carrying nature's power mistakenly injures the light, the backlash will also take my own life. This is an unbreakable, ancient covenant.

And when I finally fall, my body returning to dust, my cherished bow and crossbow will lose nature's favor. They will warp and twist on the ground, solidifying into a cold revolver.

That is the form of what I once forsook. And also a silent warning left for those who come after, about choice and consequence.
star.story.role.blood_feudist:
It is the chaos within order, the loophole in the rules, the quietly festering malice on the Harpy Train.
Its identity is fake, its exhaustion and emotions are expertly mimicked performances; inside is only boundless energy and cold purpose.
It is not born for direct killing; its power stems from the mistakes and suspicions of others.
Whenever an innocent is mistakenly identified as a werewolf and killed, it can draw nourishment from the pervasive fear and distrust, gaining a rich reward.
One mistaken kill grants it the swiftness of a passing wind.
Two mistaken kills make its attacks as fierce as a rushing rain.
Three mistaken kills bring direct wealth.
Four mistaken kills push its speed to a level ordinary mortals cannot match.
When the fifth unjust soul dies by mistake, it is reborn, immune to all negative effects.
This power, grown by the hands of others, has its core effects distilled and controlled, able to be turned on or off at its will.
Its shop is twisted, offering only the purest tools: the crowbar for destruction, the key to open forbidden areas, and the Psycho Mode that unleashes all restraint, which it can enter more frequently.
It waits, it instigates, it celebrates every tragic, self-destructive slaughter, growing stronger with each.
star.story.role.photographer:
A photographer obsessed with 'recording the truth' boarded the train.
His equipment always includes a worn work manual, filled with notes about timing and clues.
In specific corners of the train, he can find special instant cameras and precious, dedicated photo paper.
When he raises the camera and presses the shutter, the blinding flash not only temporarily blinds the person in front but can also briefly reveal things hidden in the shadows or under disguise.
Crime scenes are his favorite 'landscapes'. He freezes every suspicious detail on photo paper as potential evidence.
Aware of his perilous situation, he must carefully protect his finished photographs.
When death comes, he can no longer guard these secrets; all the photos will scatter from his body.
Using the camera is a meticulous craft. The photo paper must be loaded correctly, focus and light adjusted, to capture the desired image.
To let others see the contents, he can also display or discard the photos.
Those photo papers that recorded the truth may also become the starting point for others to trace it.
star.story.role.avenger:
You once swore to protect someone. That was the sole reason you boarded this train.
Your gaze always followed them, regardless of whether they were good or evil. To you, they were your entire world.
Until the day you witnessed them fall before you, and your outstretched hand caught nothing.
Cold fury instantly drowned your sorrow. A heavy revolver materialized in your hand—a contract, and a curse.
At the same moment, the killer's name branded itself clearly into your mind, like a hot iron.
You are no longer anyone's guardian. From this moment, you live only for revenge.
The barrel of your gun will point towards that name until one of you is completely gone.
star.story.role.conspirator:
On this train's eternal journey, some adapt, some resist, and some choose to weave the rules.
He is not passionate about direct violence; it's too crude, too predictable.
What's truly interesting is guidance, deception, and manipulation—making the target, under their own volition, walk towards their destined end within the script he has composed.
He sees himself as the player outside the chessboard, his only joys manipulation and verification.
To this end, he carries a peculiar notebook that responds to his carefully crafted guesses about others' 'identities'.
When the pen tip writes down a name and a guessed role, if it coincides with the truth, a formless and deadly force descends, causing the target to seemingly wither away.
This power comes at a high cost, with many restrictions. He is therefore forced to simplify his equipment, focusing only on core tools.
Each guess is both an assassination and a gamble.
Guess right, and an obstacle is removed or a hypothesis verified.
Guess wrong, and the cost of error accumulates deep within the soul. Three mistakes, and the backlash of death will silently find him.
He walks in the shadows and lies, each step on a tightrope strung between trust and betrayal, weaving a deadly scheme that belongs only to him.
star.story.role.slippery_ghost:
In the gap between order and chaos, an elusive shadow silently boards the train.
It calls itself Slippery Ghost. It never truly belongs to any side, yet always profits from the chaos.
Its very existence mocks the rules, forming a silent understanding with the killers who spread death—when the train returns to darkness and silence, it will be time for it to reap its victory.

A mysterious shop, open only to it and filled with strange, deceptive goods, is the source of its power. Coins, as if attracted by its charm, automatically flow into its pocket at intervals.
It peddles troublesome tricks: a blank cartridge that temporarily 'muzzles' a hunter's gun, silently dismantling their threat.
A thick cloud of smoke that obscures vision, or even directly shatters sanity, bringing pure terror, its best tool for disrupting the battlefield.
It is proficient in killers' tricks, possessing the same lockpicks to pry open forbidden doors.
It also enjoys adding fuel to the fire, plunging entire carriages into darkness at crucial moments, pulling the curtain on true chaos.

It is not a direct executioner, but a cunning instigator and interested party, leisurely collecting its own 'reward' in the shadow of blood and fire.
star.story.role.bomber:
He was once a passenger toyed with by the world's rules, struggling to survive on the edge of order.
Until he discovered, in a hidden corner of the train, a secret shop marked with code words, open only to specific 'clients'.
This shop doesn't sell ordinary supplies, but pure chaos: grenades, attention-grabbing firecrackers, lockpicks that open any partition.
Using his accumulated, blood-stained coins, he knocked on this door, and from then on, took up the torch of chaos.
His 'apotheosis' begins with a specially made remote bomb, this dangerous piece of art is not cheap.
After a brief silence, the bomb begins to tick—a fatalist's countdown offered to the unsuspecting.
And his most 'merciful' act is to smile and personally stuff this resounding 'gift' into the arms of his comrades.
Each transfer is his most splendid 'apotheosis' anthem to this desperate journey.
star.story.role.engineer:
He was once the Harpy Train Company's top maintenance engineer, knowing every inch of pipe and every line of wiring like the back of his hand.
After the disaster, the train's systems fell into semi-paralysis. Relying on his remaining engineering knowledge and personal tools, he set up a simple but efficient engineer shop.
By completing essential maintenance tasks around the train, he can obtain and stockpile coins from the residual automated systems.
The most valuable item in his shop is the master key that opens up attendant-only areas.
He is also skilled in reinforcement techniques, using reinforcement tools to set up subtle, strengthened structures behind fragile doors, enough to withstand one rough crowbar strike.
He is an expert at setting up clever alarm traps. Once installed on a door, any attempt to force it with a crowbar will trigger a piercing alarm and instantly lock the door.
He has also created complex mechanical locks. Anyone trying to pick these locks with a lockpick must perform a delicate manual operation; failure may damage their tool. However, this defense is useless against pure violence like a crowbar.
star.story.role.boxer:
He was once a champion boxer in the limelight, reigning over his kingdom with a pair of iron fists as the crowd roared.
Until that rigged final match—the opponent's concealed weapons and the referee's blind eye—brought him down on the very stage where he sought absolute fairness.
As he lay dying, a force, a mix of unwillingness and rage, called out to the Harpy Train that travels through the cracks of reality.

The train didn't heal his wounds; instead, it forged his obsession into a new weapon: 『Iron Body』.
In desperate situations, he can focus all his will into a single point, allowing his body to enter a state of absolute defense for a brief moment.
This isn't truly becoming steel; it's using an indomitable fighting spirit to create a temporary, inviolable domain for himself.
Each use heavily drains his spirit, requiring two minutes to recover and reignite his will to fight.

On the train, he found his boxing gloves again, but their meaning has changed.
Each hit isn't just for knockback or stunning enemies, but to 'interrupt' their rhythm and the equipment they rely on.
He despises any unfair tricks or fights that depend on external tools; his fists will temporarily 'cool down' the 'abnormalities' on his opponents.

Now, he moves between carriages and platforms. His fight is no longer for championship belts or empty fame.
Each heavy blow is an inquiry into, and a practice of, the pure 'fairness' he believes in.
With this once-defeated body, he continues to seek the answer he failed to find in the old world, on this new stage.
star.story.role.postman:
Before order collapsed, he was just an ordinary postman.
The Harpy Train incident swept him up, and he picked up his most familiar job.
In his bag, he always kept some blank envelopes.
At the train's supply points, he could exchange the resources he'd saved for these envelopes.
A single letter represented a safe exchange of items, protected by the rules.
He sealed his item into the envelope, handed it to another passenger, and they would return one of theirs to him.
It wasn't just an exchange, but a fragile ritual of trust in this carriage full of suspicion.
He knew he was never delivering just supplies.
In an environment where trust was hard to come by, he maintained the most basic connection and hope.
star.story.role.detective:
"Those who hide secrets wrong have nowhere to run."
You started as an insignificant private detective in the city, eking out a living by finding lost items for people. Until the money house massacre case was hastily closed, everyone insisting bandits were the real killers, you alone saw through the key and hidden ledger among the evidence bags, recognizing the frame-up.
You understood: all conspiracies, truths, and cards are hidden in backpacks. You have seen good and evil in people's baggage and are not bound by predetermined conclusions. You are the only one capable of breaking open lies in a world full of them.
Remember: it's never about what he says, but what's in his backpack.
star.story.role.stalker:
"It's either erupt in silence or perish in it."
Because I was quiet, my classmates ostracized me. Because of that, I could only pass my time after class reading books. My silence gave them the capital to bully me.
I wanted friends too... Why are you doing this to me... I could only sit in my seat watching them play together, my only companion my own shadow. I hate them. I hate everyone.
I boarded a train to leave this place. On board, I met a medical student. He shared his past with me. I saw disappointment in humanity in his eyes. He also saw the thirst for blood in mine.
From then on, I had a new name, what he always called me: 『Stalker』. I will watch you, with a blade forged from your hatred, let you experience the silence after death.
Now, it's my turn to speak...
star.story.role.athlete:
He was once the darling of the spotlight, chasing fractions of seconds and the roar of the crowd on racetracks.
After a strange training accident, he found himself on a strange, endless train.
There were no tracks here, no audience, only endless danger and the instinct to survive.
Perhaps the years of grueling training had reshaped his body, for he found himself possessing near-limitless stamina.
Running, jumping, dodging—those physical abilities that once earned him glory were now his greatest assets for survival.
In desperate moments, he could recall the feeling of wind rushing past his ears as he sprinted, suddenly bursting with incredible speed.
This power couldn't last; he needed time for his boiling blood to cool again.
On this train, he was no longer an athlete vying for medals, but a fugitive fighting to survive, forced to run forever.
star.story.role.admirer:
On the Harpy Train, there are beings known as 'Observers'. They harbor a hidden, fervent obsession with acts of killing.
They are not born killers, just some whose admiration for the power and spectacle of ending a life has become twisted and distorted.
At first, they can only hide in the shadows, satisfying their restless cravings through a prolonged, almost pathological, staring.
As their gaze fixes intensely on a specific target for a long time, a strange power begins to brew and well up inside them.
This energy, absorbed through voyeurism, doesn't stem from love, but from a sick resonance with and imitation of the act of 'ending'.
When this accumulated energy breaks through a critical threshold, a qualitative change occurs.
The 'Observer' completes their final metamorphosis. No longer a mere onlooker, they become a manipulator capable of truly 'intervening' and 'guiding'.
From this moment, their goal aligns completely with that of the true harvesters on the train. No longer just 'appreciating', they now personally participate, ensuring the bloody drama concludes the way they (or the killers they identify with) wish it to.
star.story.role.trapper:
Born in the dark pipes and abandoned carriages of the train, a hunter skilled in treachery.
It is not the strongest monster, but the most patient trap-maker.
Its very existence is a silent desecration and twisting of the rule-defined space.
It can condense its own malice into invisible omens of calamity, silently etched onto the passenger paths.
These omens blend perfectly with the shadows and noises of the environment, unseen until triggered by the unfortunate target.
Once triggered, a loud noise and a flash of light instantly tear through the silence, exposing the victim's panic and location.
More deadly, the omen imprisons the trigger-happy fool in fear, rendering them immobile for a short time, easy prey.
And the fool who steps into the same nightmare multiple times faces exponentially increasing punishment, the imprisonment stretching into hopeless despair.
It relishes the process of setting up and waiting, watching its prey walk step by step into its cunning net.
Each successful imprisonment is a victory for it, a declaration of its will over the twisted order of this space.
Its traps are its extended will, sowing silent misfortune in this steel prison.
star.story.role.star:
She is the shining idol from the Harpy Train, once the brightest light on screens and stages.
Her light is not an illusion but an ability to solidify popularity and connections into a strange kind of bond.
She can personally sign her name for a fellow passenger. This signature becomes an invisible beacon, creating a faint link between her and her follower, allowing the follower to sense her location.
She can also sign a more solemn, more ancient contract with a trusted passenger—a life-and-death pact.
When a passenger who has signed such a pact faces a fatal crisis, the contract is triggered.
The fan's fervent belief and the idol's protective will transform into a power that reverses causality, blocking the fatal blow for the protected.
However, the gifts of fate are priced beforehand; the cost of this protection is extremely heavy: the damage is entirely transferred, causing the star idol to receive the fatal blow in their stead.
Behind the brilliant light stands her resolve, ready to be extinguished for trust and connection.
star.story.role.veteran:
I am the last one.
Of the thirty-seven in my company, only I came back alive. Along with this saber—one of my comrades pushed it into my hand as he died. Its hilt still has a piece of shrapnel embedded in it.
Now, someone else has died in this carriage. I reach for my waist. The sound of the blade leaving its sheath is exactly the same as it was in those trenches.
The killer is in the shadows.
I grip the hilt—not to kill anyone. It's just a habit. In the darkness, to grip something.
star.story.role.singer:
I am just an ordinary singer. No weapons, no fighting skills. I can't even offer a single word of real comfort.
I gently pluck the strings. The first note is very faint, lost in the noise. The second, the third... slowly, the melody unfolds. It's the oldest lullaby this border town knows. Gentle, slow, like snow falling on a barren plain, like wind blowing through the trees.
I am just an ordinary singer. I don't know where the killer is hiding, I don't know what will happen next.
All I know is, on this train heading into the unknown, amidst death and panic, all I can do is finish this song. The strings don't stop, the voice doesn't break. Until the carriage falls silent again, with only the wheels and the rails gently humming along to my melody.
star.settings.role.singer:
In the dark night roads and unpopulated forests, there are many youkai that use songs to enchant humans. Mystia Lorelei is one of them.

Her appearance has never been seen, only her song, which can be heard from anywhere. If you get lost while enchanted by that song, you will be attacked by a youkai.
There are few confirmed sightings during the day, but she is said to be a large, winged humanoid youkai (×1 If she looked like that from the start, she probably wouldn't be called a Night Sparrow.).

Mystia's songs are surprisingly flashy, with an excellent rhythm quite unlike a youkai's song. Because of this, they are popular among the youth (×2 Even though they shouldn't be popular.).
In contrast, the older generation of singing youkai say things like, "Lately, the young youkai just make noise, singing songs we don't understand all day. It's very troubling. Isn't that just noise?"

Also, to make it difficult for those lured by her song to see their surroundings, she has the ability to give humans night blindness. If you walk on a dark road at night guided only by limited lamplight and a song, you will eventually be attacked by this youkai.


# Witness Reports

I thought the Prismriver Ensemble had hired a singer, but nothing good happened when I got closer. (Anonymous)
