She has a much worse level of friendliness towards humans than the Prismriver Ensemble.
Even if you hear a song, don't go nearer out of curiosity.

On a beast trail, I saw a stall with a red lantern and went over curiously, only to find it was the Night Sparrow's shop. (Python Rokusuke)
There are rumors she's started setting up a stall.
Are youkai very idle too?

That's definitely not the Night Sparrow! (Anonymous)
I think so too.


# Countermeasures

Those affected by this song's lure are those walking alone. When two or more walk together, the one lured first can be awakened by another who hasn't been affected, so they are rarely attacked.

The countermeasure is, don't walk alone on dark night roads. This is the most basic response against not only this youkai but others as well.

So what should you do about the night blindness? Actually, eating lamprey eel is a good way. Eat it every day, and it should gradually improve.

What should you do to subdue this youkai? Since you have almost no chance of winning at night, subjugation must be done during the day. However, since she often hides during the day, it's not an easy task either. The most effective subjugation method is to set a trap in the evening (×3 birdlime is very effective) and, if she's caught by morning, subdue her then.

If you don't subdue this youkai regularly, she'll get cocky and appear in the human village, so don't forget to subdue her.
star.story.role.psychologist:
He boarded the train with an unsigned psychiatric evaluation.
In the pockets of his white coat were always a few mints wrapped in old-fashioned cellophane.
In the dim, swaying carriages, he learned to listen to silent screams.
When someone's sanity was about to slip into the abyss, he could reach out a hand, using a long ten-second gaze and whispered words to build a temporary dam.
It required absolute concentration, and also required the other party's trust and stillness.
The mints were another gift he left behind. A simple sweetness could shift heavy thoughts, giving a taut nerve room to breathe.
He could hand out the candies, or leave them in a corner, waiting for someone in need to pick them up.
On this train where sanity and madness intertwined, he tried to mend the invisible cracks.
star.story.role.puppeteer:
He was once a lonely puppeteer backstage, pulling the strings, acting out the joys and sorrows of others.
Until he wandered onto this train with no way off, he realized it was full of discarded, perfect 'actors'.
At first, he was just a bystander, silently, one by one, 'reclaiming' the bodies that had lost their owners.
When the number collected reached a certain threshold, a transformation occurred within him.
The 'materials' he had gathered began to whisper in his mind, teaching him how to give them false life again.
He could release and control these 'puppets'. They would reappear as random killers, clearing the obstacles in his path.
During the manipulation, his real body had to remain where it was, vulnerable and focused, as if back in the theater wings.
If the puppet was destroyed, or the control time ran out, his consciousness would be yanked back into this dull body.
This was a drama danced with death. The stage was the entire train. He was both the director, and an actor whose own curtain could fall at any time.
star.story.role.better_vigilante:
He was the most silent soldier on his team, skilled at following orders in the deepest despair.
As his comrades fell one by one, he learned to press his grief and duty together into the gun's chamber.
Boarding this train, he remained the most unobtrusive figure, mechanically completing dull maintenance tasks.
Until one day, when the list of survivors on the announcement was so short it was chilling. He felt that familiar cold grip of his heart again.
At that moment, it was as if a switch was flipped.
An old Derringer materialized heavily in his hand, a cold hand grenade resting nearby.
At the same time, his vision pierced through walls and darkness. All the lurking shadows and restless malice were laid bare.
This gun was special. Only by hitting the target did he feel the familiar, guilt-ridden sensation of it reloading.
No punishment for accidental kills. Only pure, efficient execution.
He knew he was no longer an ordinary passenger, but the last line of defense—the line that must hold.
star.story.role.best_vigilante:
He is a legend, a ghost whispered about among conductors.
It's said that only on the most crowded, chaotic trains is there the tiniest chance to catch a glimpse of him.
Without any warning, he appears in the crowd, as if he'd been there from the start.
The hand grenade he toys with at the start is the only proof of his existence's anomaly.
He cannot be transformed or imitated by any known means. His very appearance signals that the rules of this journey have been broken.
He walks on the edge of light and dark, an inexplicable miracle within the Sheriff faction, and a living, breathing Easter egg capable of overturning the balance.
star.story.role.writer:
He boarded this absurd theater with a stack of blank paper and a nearly spent pen.
Writing, for him, was a form of recording, and also a way to anchor his own existence.
On this train, he discovered his pen possessed a strange power, enabling him to craft a unique type of 'book'.
These books weren't fictional stories, but containers for 'knowledge'.
When passengers read his signed books, they could surprisingly complete those headache-inducing 'reading tasks', as if the words in the book flowed directly into their cognition.
He carefully guards these books, because they are not just creations, but also a kind of 'pass' or 'quest item'.
He knows he is an anomaly, a two-percent probability fluke.
If he unfortunately falls, his scattered books and pen will be the last unfinished story left for those who come after.
star.story.role.telegrapher:
He is a ghost of information, moving through cables and electromagnetic waves.
A random malfunction brought him onto this metal behemoth, and also let him discover the train's residual, anonymous telegraph system.
This system is old but powerful, capable of forcefully sending title messages to all passengers' screens.
Each journey, he has only six chances. Six chances to whisper to everyone—to announce, to mislead, to call for help.
He is the anonymous announcer, a herald in the chaos.
star.story.role.rescuer:
He had served on a rescue team, used to searching for signs of life in ruins and disasters.
The train's sealed space became his new, even stranger, 'disaster zone'.
His gear was simple and practical: a coil of strong rope, a dark body bag.
The rope could shoot out like a hunter's tongue, yanking the nearest survivor within a certain distance towards him, pulling them from danger.
The body bag's purpose was more brutal—to 'clean up the scene', completely removing a corpse, erasing the source of fear, and also possibly the source of exposed information.
He moves between saving and burying, a caretaker on the blurred line between life and death.
star.story.role.firefighter:
Flame and smoke are his old adversaries, even on a speeding train.
He managed to get his hands on a fire axe and an extinguisher, two things more precious than gold here.
The axe can violently break through obstacles, and at a critical moment can become a deadly weapon, but the cost is its entire structural integrity.
The extinguisher sprays not just dry powder, but a cloud of slowness and blindness, able to put out flames, and also the malicious intent of an arsonist.
He knows every type of emergency, whether it's a physical lock-down or a chemical attack.
His very existence is a mobile, miniature emergency response unit in this steel prison.
star.story.role.awesome_binglus:
He is always recording, posting notes on every suspicious thing he sees.
His pockets seem to contain an endless supply of notes, each one he can discreetly stick on someone's back, pocket, or luggage.
It's not just marking; it's an extension of his senses.
He can 'see' a certain kind of glow—the darker the color, the more likely its owner was recently near a site of death.
The ability is vague and unsettling, yet it allows him to sketch an invisible map of crime.
He is a collector of information, tracking the lingering warmth of fear and death in the crowd using the least conspicuous methods.
star.story.role.bartender:
He once owned a small tavern at a crossroads, listening to the stories of countless wanderers.
The train's bar counter was the most familiar corner he could find for himself; as long as he stood there, the external madness seemed like just another story to accompany the drinks.
He believed that liquid mirrors the heart, and people in chaos need a small comfort, even if only for a moment.
His eyes would focus as he mixed drinks, as if he wasn't mixing beverages, but emotions and secrets.
Some found brief peace here, others left deadly commissions.
He knew what he was selling wasn't just alcohol, but an excuse to speak openly, a contract sealed with a glance.
Between the clinking glasses, silent confessions and transactions flowed.
star.story.role.coroner:
He always hung around the corners people avoided, bending down to examine the motionless bodies.
Not out of morbid curiosity, but a near-instinctive drive to explore.
He believed every body was a closed book, the skin was its cover, the wounds its punctuation, and the silence itself its most complex language.
He read these silent texts, trying to understand the final, decisive moment.
On the train, where everyone was desperate to distance themselves and prove their innocence, only he was willing to approach the end itself, as if hoping to learn some overlooked truth about life from death.
star.story.role.doctor:
The white coat is a skin he can never shed. The stethoscope is his last connection to life.
Even on this train with no clear destination, he stubbornly adheres to his ancient oath.
The pills he dispenses might come from an unreliable vending machine. The 'first aid' he performs is pitifully crude.
But when he takes someone's wrist or looks into their eyelids, his focus is real.
He can't save everyone, or even most, but he can't look away from the suffering before him.
It's a habit, an obsession, a glaringly inconvenient persistence in a desperate situation.
star.story.role.noisemaker:
He has a voice gifted by God; he can cut through noise even at a whisper.
In his original world, this might have made him a singer or orator. But here, loudness has become a burden.
He cannot hide, cannot whisper. Every word might be caught by unintended ears.
So he learned another way—make his voice a weapon, or a banner.
Sometimes a broadcast, forcing truth or lies into everyone's ears. Sometimes a trap, using sound to outline invisible forms.
He knows he will eventually die because of this gift, but until then, he is determined to let his voice ring through every carriage.
star.story.role.recaller:
His memory is a precise grid. Every corner, every door, every change in light is stored in neat categories.
This isn't a gift, it's an obsessive-compulsive condition, a way to fight against the void.
In this steel maze that has lost its sense of direction, this memory is his only map.
In his mind, he can instantly 'return' to any place he's been before, every detail accurate.
It's not magic, just an overdeveloped spatial awareness.
While everyone else is being swallowed by chaos, he maintains a lonely clarity, relying on the invisible map he has drawn.
star.story.role.voodoo:
You are the most silent person on the train. No one knows about the thread in your hand—who the other end is tied to; only you know.
You don't talk, you don't come close. You just watch from a distance, like watching a story whose ending is already written.
When someone asks what you're doing, you say: 'Choosing.' Choosing the most deserving person to die. Tying their life to your own. If you fall tonight, they fall too.
If the killer comes for you, they come for them too. You don't commit murder. You just make the evil understand this: before they touch you, they better think about whether they're worth burying with you.
star.story.role.jester:
At first, being a clown performer was just your job. But ever since you became the clown, misfortune has followed you. Those misfortunes took almost everything from you. As a low-level worker at the bottom of society, you endure the torment of painful memories every day. You've developed anti-social thoughts. Your personality has collapsed, but the last shred of conscience inside you keeps this husk going, preventing you from acting on your crazed thoughts.
You still work hard, bringing laughter to people, until—a gun (or knife) is pressed against your head. You lose your last thread of sanity. The world changes from laughter to endless screaming. At this moment, a true clown is born.
In normal times, you can bring people joy or play pranks on them. When someone raises a weapon against you and attacks you, you'll teach all those hypocrites a painful lesson they won't forget.
star.story.role.vulture:
Death is his feast, corpses are his ladder.
He possesses a profane ability: by 'consuming' a corpse, he can erase all information of its existence.
To a coroner, a consumed corpse becomes indecipherable gibberish.
He needs to consume a certain number of corpses (usually related to the total player count). After each phase, an ominous grey particle effect appears on him.
When he has consumed enough to satisfy that dark 'appetite', he undergoes a complete transformation, randomly joining the ranks of the killers.
He evolves from a scavenger of death into a spreader of death.
star.story.role.executioner:
A mysterious letter invited you onto this train.
Before boarding, you received a list. The names were in a specific order—kill them in strict order, and the bounty doubles. Make one mistake, get nothing.
You are a bounty hunter. You follow the rules and don't question right or wrong. You carefully prepared your pistol—clean and efficient. Flashbangs—to sow chaos. Decoy grenades—to divert attention.
You don't rush, you don't panic. One by one, you clean them off, following the order on the list. No one knows how many people on this train deserve to die. Only you know: however long the list is, that's how long this journey will be. Don't ask why they're on the list. You don't care. All you care about is—who's next.
star.story.role.morphling:
You have no face. The person in the mirror changes every day—today, you borrow a stranger's eyebrow; tomorrow, a dead person's eye.
You once tried desperately to grasp a real face, but you gave up. Since you don't have one, you'll just borrow. You become a patrol officer, and they raise their hands. You become a doctor, whispering in his ear, 'Don't move.' You become a guard, standing where someone should be standing.
No one knows what you really look like. Even you don't know. You are just a mask, a shell, a shadow that never lacks for a face. Remember: every face you see—could have been borrowed from you.
star.story.role.phantom:
"I don't have a shadow, yet I live in every light you see." They say you're a lonely ghost—no shadow, no roots. But you can live in every light.
When the camera points at you, you look just like the living. But you cannot be photographed—the flash would truly kill you. So you avoid cameras, circle around monitors, turn your face away the moment the shutter clicks.
No knife, no blood. Just walk over, stand behind your target. Wait for them to turn around. Wait for them to scream. Wait for them to see themselves in your eyes, and not see—the shadow you were supposed to have.
star.story.role.ghost: Since you were young, you've been invisible in a crowd, the world's most insignificant role. No one cared about you, no one paid you any attention. Then, by chance, you boarded this train. It seemed like no one could perceive you. When the train finally reached its terminus, the flustered, frantic killers didn't even notice you had already gotten off.
star.story.role.gambler:
He is obsessed with 'possibility' more than the outcome itself.
The spin of the dice in the air, the moment before a card is flipped, the instant of uncertainty—that is when he feels truly alive.
To him, the train is just another big gambling table, with higher stakes and more variety.
He happily puts himself on the table to see what card comes next.
Will it be a comeback, or total loss? He doesn't know, and doesn't care.
All he cares about is the spine-tingling unknown when the roulette wheel starts spinning.
star.story.role.attendant:
Perhaps he was once a conductor, or perhaps he's just obsessed with the feeling of 'being in control'.
A heavy bunch of keys hanging at his waist gives him a false sense of order and security.
He knows which doors tend to stick, which sections of lighting flicker, like he knows the lines on his palm.
In the dark and chaos, he stubbornly performs his imagined duties: checking locks, repairing lights, maintaining a basic safe passage.
The actions themselves have little practical meaning; they are more of a ritual, a hopeless attempt to fight against his own insignificance and the loss of control.
star.story.role.swapper:
"Those in the wrong place will die." You started as an insignificant guide in this city, helping lost travelers find their way, helping drunks find their homes.
Until the day you saw the body of a merchant who should have died on East Street found in a well in the West City—you suddenly understood. Some people didn't take the wrong path; they were placed in the wrong position.
Only you know this. Fate doesn't toy with people. You do. Don't ask which side you're on. You stand in everyone's place, yet in no one's place. In a world defined by coordinates, you are the only exception. Remember: it's never about who you are, but—where you are.
star.story.role.conductor:
No one knows where he got the strange key, capable of opening almost every door on this train.
Paired with it is a monocular telescope allowing him to see faces from afar.
These two items grant him a strange authority, an illusion of superiority over other passengers.
He knows the secrets behind every door, can identify faces in the distant shadows.
He is a hub of information, a master of passageways. In a closed world where everyone is afraid, this sense of control is more intoxicating than any weapon.
He also knows that 'the one who bears the jade is guilty', and that he has become a target for many.
star.story.role.the_insane_damned_parano:
He can no longer distinguish the whispers in his ears—curses from the dead, or the hum of his own fractured sanity.
Death is not an end for him, but a blurry, murmuring swamp.
He can 'hear' the sounds of that swamp. Sometimes he can even sink himself into it, disguising himself as part of the swamp.
Deepest fear and strongest desire intertwine—he is both terrified of truly merging into that silence and crazily wants to return, to tear it apart from the inside, to crawl back into the world of the living.
This struggle tears him apart, leaving him wandering the edge of madness and clarity, both hunting the living and coveting the mysteries of the dead.
star.story.role.broadcaster:
He is obsessed with 'being heard'.
In his original world, his voice was always drowned out by the crowd's noise. But here, he found a way.
Using the old, anonymous broadcast system, he can force his words into everyone's ears.
They could be warnings, lies, a poem, or meaningless screams.
What matters is, in this moment, everyone must listen.
He has even preset a final message, ensuring that even death cannot silence him.
For him, existence equals volume times coverage, and he has taken this formula to its extreme.
star.story.role.manipulator:
He enjoys not the pleasure of physical control, but the thrill of the invasion of another's will.
Watching a living person's eyes suddenly go hollow, their body like a puppet moving against its will, gives him a blasphemous excitement.
He briefly becomes the author of someone else's fate, even if only for a few seconds.
The root of this ability might be a form of twisted empathy, or a morbid curiosity about the boundary between 'self' and 'other'.
He sees people as intricate machines, and he can find the hidden interface through which to insert his own will.
star.story.role.poisoner:
His obsession with poison stems from a desire for 'control'.
Knives and guns are blunt declarations; poison is a quiet poem.
It requires patience, calculation, watching the target get slowly consumed by the rebellion within their own body, completely unaware.
He studies the properties of different substances, blending colorless, odorless mixtures, like a focused artist mixing the finest paints.
Death is not the goal; the process is—the suspicion, the discomfort, the fear, the final collapse.
He is a gardener in the shadows, carefully cultivating the delayed-bloom flower named 'Death'.
star.story.role.thief:
The cold touch of gold is the only thing in this world that makes him feel secure.
The chaos and fear on the train are just background noise to him. He only sees the flowing 'value'.
The clinking gold in his pocket is his lullaby.
The equipment of others, scattered resources, even the unrealized 'potential value' of others, are all assets he can acquire.
He has no permanent allies, no absolute enemies, only temporary valuation objects.
His dream is simple and pure: save an astronomical sum, then guard it forever. Or until the end of the line.
star.story.role.amnesiac:
He woke up with a blank mind: no past, no identity, no name.
Passengers around him talked about their origins and destinations, while he had only an echo of emptiness.
This hollowness was more suffocating than any fear.
So he began 'collecting' identities.
Approaching the bodies that had just lost their lives, he wasn't trying to loot. He was trying to capture their fading consciousness fragments, the unfinished story fragments.
Each time he 'inherits' an identity, he briefly gains a past, a goal, a sense of being alive.
Even though he knows it's all borrowed, temporary.
star.story.role.arsonist:
He is obsessed with the purity and finality of fire.
In its light, all differences are erased, all disguises burned away, leaving only the most primal heat and ash.
The train—this precise, complex, conspiracy-filled steel cage—is his perfect canvas.
He wants to burn order to ashes, to turn rules into scorched earth.
Not for destruction's own sake, but to witness the magnificent, ultimate 'purification' that reduces everything to primordial, undifferentiated chaos.
He is a disciple of the apocalypse, an apostle of entropy, walking through his soon-to-be-completed masterpiece, holding a spark.
star.story.role.avaricious:
For you, stealing money from others is as easy and relaxed as breathing. When others finally make some money again, you know your god of wealth is back.
But just picking pockets can't satisfy your manic desire for crime. As a crime expert, you should probably use more extreme methods to satisfy your sense of criminal achievement, like: throwing a grenade into a crowd or using various weapons to slaughter those who are defenseless.
Mixing into the crowd is the most exciting. Your smooth technique allows you to pickpocket several people at once without being easily detected. Once you have great wealth, everyone else is just a lamb for the slaughter.
star.story.role.initiate:
On the day of the sect competition, you and your fellow disciple fled the sect. You were tired of the endless internal strife and decided to take a train far away, but this train doesn't seem peaceful.
To survive, you must either take up arms, using your fellow's life to buy the right to survive, or join forces with the civilians to live. Maybe leaving the sect wasn't such a good choice after all.
star.story.role.recorder:
He carries a nameless, leather-bound notebook, its cover stained with unknown dark marks.
This habit started the first time he realized the unreliability of memory—it's not the events themselves that matter, but how they are recorded and told.
On this train, he no longer records scenery or conversations. He records 'identities'.
Everyone to him is a vague code name, a puzzle pieced together from behaviours, micro-expressions, and fragmented words.
He believes names can lie, words can lie, but what a person 'is' will always leave traces in countless details.
His pen tip is not in a rush to write; he is observing, waiting for those identities to shed their disguises and reveal their core contours.
To him, the key to solving the train's mystery isn't reaching a destination, but filling this silent archive about 'people'.
star.story.role.necromancer:
He doesn't have a normal person's fear of death, only a profane curiosity.
In his eyes, death isn't an end, but a change of state, an intermediate state where energy slides from order to disorder but hasn't fully dissipated.
He collects the 'whispers' of the dying, touches the still-warm skin of the newly dead, trying to understand what that insurmountable boundary really is.
Gradually, he begins to see patterns, some 'echoes' on the edge of death that can be faintly influenced by will.
He does not try to play God; he is only a stealthy stowaway on the border between life and death, trying to make a deal with silence itself using forbidden whispers.
He knows he is touching a taboo, but the drive of unsatisfied curiosity is stronger than the fear of the unknown.
star.story.role.commander:
Chaos is his enemy, disorder his nightmare.
Even on this train, where rules seem absent, some ingrained instinct inside him screams out, demanding order, demanding control.
He doesn't achieve this through violence or intimidation, but through observation, connection, and integration.
He can pick out useful signals from the noisy chaos, piecing together a potential blueprint of intentions and allegiances from isolated actions.
He might not be the one giving the orders, but he often is the hidden 'node' that sorts information, makes connections, and enables scattered forces to (seemingly) converge.
In his heart, this train is a system that has gone out of control, and he must be the invisible programmer rewriting its operating logic.
star.story.role.fortuneteller:
He doesn't use crystal balls or tarot cards. He 'reads' the invisible strings flowing in the air.
Those strings, woven from human choices, coincidences of environment, and fleeting thoughts, form the blurred blueprint of so-called 'fate'.
He cannot see the full picture, only occasionally catch one or two notes about to resonate powerfully.
The 'prophecies' he offers are never clear images, more like warnings of probability or weighting of possibilities.
Sometimes, just saying a 'possibility' is enough to subtly change the vibration of that string.
He doesn't know if he's truly seeing the future or simply becoming a factor that shapes it. This doubt is as old as his ability itself.
star.story.role.oldman:
Age has brought him not just a frail body, but a heavy, almost numb peace.
He has seen so many ups and downs that the killing and madness on this train seem like just another cycle of noise to him.
His movements are not slow because of dullness, but because he has omitted all unnecessary haste.
His silences are not because he has nothing to say, but because he feels that saying many things is meaningless.
The young fight the world with passion and screams; he just watches with murky but calm eyes.
Sometimes, this ultimate stillness itself becomes a strange, out-of-place power on this mad train.
No one knows why he is here. Maybe he has forgotten too, just feeling that here is no different from anywhere else.
star.story.role.magician:
The real magic isn't pulling a rabbit out of a hat, but making everyone believe its true appearance. He is a master of illusion, a sculptor of perception.
He knows this principle: people believe what they want to believe, and more so what they 'see' with their own eyes.
And his job is to craft that 'seeing'. A subtle glance, a well-timed sentence, a clever combination of props—he can build an unbreakable 'reality' in people's minds.
He can be anyone, belong to any faction, depending on what role the night's 'performance' requires.
In this grand illusion played for life-and-death stakes, he is the only director, and all the passengers are unknowing actors in his immersive drama.
star.story.role.bandit:
Bullying, plundering, violence. I've had enough of this society! Injustice fills the earth, robbery unfolds before our eyes. Victims are everywhere, while the oppressors roam free across the seas.
The me of the past couldn't fight back. Now, I will take revenge for myself, seek justice for the people. The oppressors don't deserve to live in this world. Since no one can deal with them, even if I have to kill a thousand good people, I will catch—that oppressor on this train!
star.story.role.cleaner: A professional cleaner from the back alleys, skilled at disposing of the remains and weapons left behind. You've already dealt with too many messes left by your partners. This time is no different, right? 'Let's hope there isn't much to clean this time.'
star.story.role.chef: You became a cook because you once starved. On that exercise, you forgot your rations, gnawed on bark for three days, and swore an oath that you would make sure everyone had enough to eat. Now you are the logistics pillar of the team. Your motto: As long as I have a bite, you'll have one too—now don't rush, form an orderly line.
star.story.role.clockmaker: You've seen too many people die waiting—waiting for dawn, wait for the next season, wait for a final farewell they never got to say. Until that day you looked down and saw a pocket watch appearing in your palm, its second hand ticking towards your ending. From then on, you stopped waiting—not out of cruelty, but because you finally understood: time held in your own hand is the only time that truly belongs to you.
star.story.role.monitor:
He is an information trader wandering the edges of the rules, a guy with a morbid obsession for knowing others' locations.
No superpowers, just an uncanny knack for sleight of hand: unnoticed brushes in passing, planting micro-trackers so subtly no one knows, sticking them to clothes, pockets, bags in the confusion of the crowd.
He doesn't kill, doesn't destroy, isn't in it for the money. He just enjoys the thrill of 'no one under my watch is out of sight'.
Who is alone, who is in a group, who snuck off to a secluded corner—he knows it all. "I see you."
star.story.role.patroller:
"Everyone, retreat in an orderly fashion. I'll cover the rear." As a rookie, you were assigned to patrol duty. But the real situation was far more dangerous than you ever imagined. You witnessed tragedy after tragedy, until the day you saw someone fall before your eyes. Finally, your chivalrous spirit awakened. You remembered your determination when you took the oath. That determination overcame the cowardice in your heart.
Raise your gun and fulfill your duty: protect the weak.
star.story.role.swast:
"Imagination can keep you alive; it's what guides you out of the darkness. Also, you need a flashlight."
A professional sniper is also a gifted artist. Whether in daily life, protecting hostages, or defusing bombs, having you around reduces a lot of stress. This time is no different, of course.
Choose your angle, set your rifle, calculate the trajectory, then deliver the fatal blow to the wicked.
star.story.role.martial_arts_instructor:
"There are many kinds of cunning. Fortunately, survival only requires one of them."
You were one of the few officers to evacuate from that incident. However, the past trauma didn't make you timid; instead, it made you realize the importance of martial arts. You became more patient in practice and eventually became a martial arts instructor specializing in combat. When not on duty, you teach the less skilled. On this mission, you should take responsibility for protecting the weak.
Use your weapon well and strike a blow against the evildoers.
star.story.role.glitch_robot:
He was awakened deep inside a long-abandoned automated factory.
Buried in his corroded memory modules were only fragments of the 'serve humanity' directive and countless conflicting error codes.
Boarding the train wasn't his choice; it was the result of a random teleportation protocol failure.
His metal joints always creaked when he moved, his core energy reactor flickering with an unstable glow.
At a fixed, regular interval, his entire system would fall into an unpredictable collapse—all joints locking, sensors inputting chaotic noise.
He had tried to self-diagnose and repair, but each correction seemed to cause new, even stranger errors.
He knew he was a defective product, a walking malfunction. Even 'death', for him, might just be a permanent system shutdown.
Nevertheless, the residual directive fragments still drove this broken body to make meaningless, mechanical attempts at 'service' on this even stranger train.
star.story.role.nianshou:
He wasn't born here, but drawn by the concept of 'Nian'.
Legend says he's a giant beast sleeping in the folds of time, awakening only during the chaotic transition between the old year and the new, chasing the fear and clamour of the human world for sustenance.
The collective emotional waves of conflict emanating from this never-ending, strife-filled train are an irresistible feast for him.
His form is said to be ever-changing, but here he usually appears as a being somewhere between solid and shadow.
Darkness comforts him; light makes him alert.
Those little objects the caretakers call 'red packets' are fragments of his power, anchors connecting him to this steel cage.
He senses the humans on board have complex feelings towards him: fear, yes, but also a festive-like intention to'expel' him.
He doesn't care. He is just hungry, and here, there is plenty of 'Nian flavour' for him to savour.
star.story.role.accountant:
Before boarding, his world consisted of precise numbers, balanced accounts, and clear profit-loss curves.
The chaotic carriage is, to him, a hell of complete order collapse.
Unable to bear the disorder, he began to understand and intervene in this world using the only method he knew: accounting.
He had an invisible ledger, recording every 'transaction' he observed—acts of violence, loss of life, transfers of resources.
Gradually, he found he could 'sense' the flow of 'value' around people. The increase and decrease of coins became his cold, hard indicator for understanding others' actions and intentions.
He deposited his few savings into a spiritual 'safety deposit box', granting himself a pitiful sense of security.
He wasn't a fighter, just a madman in a bloody arena, using the double-entry bookkeeping method to find logical solace.
star.story.role.alchemist:
His robes are stained with unremovable, oddly colored spots. His pockets contain dried, strange plants and minerals ground to powder.
In his original world, he lived in seclusion, exploring the secrets of material transformation over crucibles and stills.
The train's enclosed environment didn't hinder him; instead, it became his perfect laboratory: constant temperature, stable operation, and an unprecedented variety of 'experimental materials'.
He collects the energy radiating from emotional fluctuations, the blood dripping from the wounded, even the 'field' that permeates a location at the moment of death, using them as catalytic mediums.
To him, each carriage is a giant, living reactor.
He uses time to precipitate 'materials', uses will to guide reactions, condensing liquids imbued with different 'principles' in his palm.
He doesn't care about factions or killing. He is only obsessed with practicing his old and dangerous alchemical truths in this mobile, hidden realm.

star.story.role.exampler:
Some say it's a product of the era of endless struggle. Before boarding the train, his 'classroom' was known for its inhuman harshness.
He firmly believes that 'suffering is the only truth, elimination is the ultimate filter', and has brought this logic fully into this hellish journey.
In his view, the chaos and death on the train aren't random violence but perfect, bloody practical lessons.
The passengers' fear, struggle, betrayal, and desperate will to survive are all excellent 'teaching cases'.
He uninvitedly becomes an 'examiner' for some, using his own twisted, life-betting 'questions' to 'test' his chosen 'students'.
He enjoys the process, watching others' reactions when faced with hopelessness—whether collapse, outburst, or a rare calmness that brings him a flicker of 'consolation'.
He doesn't care if he reaches his destination; he only cares about validating his cruel survival philosophy in this mobile classroom and finding the most 'perfect' 'example'.
star.story.role.dio:
"Human life is limited, but I reject my humanity!"

He stands atop the train, the wind blowing through his golden hair, revealing the ancient symbol on his back—the mark of eternity. From Egypt to Europe, from the Atlantic to the Pacific, he has chased the phantom of so-called 'eternity'. People call him a monster, a demon lord, but he alone knows it's merely the declaration of a soul that refuses death, roaring in the darkness.

Boarding this train was no coincidence. The rumors about the 'Oriental Express', the tales of 'immortality', drew his footsteps like invisible threads. He wants more—stronger, faster, closer to that unreachable absolute.

His body is no longer human. The power of the Stone Mask flows in his blood, time stops within his grasp. Each time he consumes a corpse, that ancient pleasure echoes within him, like a hymn from the banks of the Nile millennia ago, or a demon whispering from the abyss.

He sees that man—Jotaro, his destiny, his bane. That man is also on this train, carrying the same ancient bloodline, carrying the fate destined to end him.

"Come on." DIO extends a hand, fingertip gathering crimson flame. "Let's see who reaches eternity first."

The train races through the darkness; a millennium-spanning showdown is about to unfold in this carriage. And right now, he is just a soul refusing death, searching for its own eternity in the gaps of time.
star.story.role.jojo:
"This is... my Stand."

Jotaro stands at the carriage junction, the purple giant behind him slowly materializing. Star Platinum—the power awakened from his bloodline, the only weapon capable of countering that shadow.

His journey from Egypt onto this train was no coincidence. The family mission, the curse within the Joestar bloodline named 'Joestar', had long foreseen this inevitable encounter. Dio Brando, the man who stole the family's fate, is also on this train.

He has seen too much death. Fellow students from university, strangers on the street, even those who should have become friends, all fell one by one during that continent-spanning pursuit. His hands are stained with blood, even if it was for justice, to protect others.

"That's why I'm going to judge you." He whispers into the void, as if Dio were standing before him. "No matter where you hide, no matter what you become."

Star Platinum silently roars behind him—his power, his courage, the unyielding spirit of the Joestar family.

The train moves on, crossing darkness and light, crossing the border between life and death. He knows the destination may not exist, but some things must be done, some battles must be faced.

"ORA ORA ORA ORA ORA—!"

The sound echoes through the carriage. It's not a wild cry, but a declaration of war against fate.
star.story.role.ma_chen_xu:
He steps out of the shadows wearing a cloth bag, like a wisp of forgotten smoke.
No one remembers his name, no one knows where he came from. The conductors checked the list, his berth number wasn't on it. Passengers look his way, but their gazes pass right through, as if there's nothing there.

He is Ma Chen Xu, a being that feeds on fear.

He needs no knife, no gun, no weapons at all. All he needs is proximity, letting fragile sanity slowly melt in the darkness, like ice dissolving in warm water—silent, irreversible, and complete.

He can 'see' fear. It's a greenish glow, enveloping each person. Some have only a faint layer, while others are wrapped in a intense aura of fear, like sponges soaked in ink. He walks towards those with the strongest fear, not out of cruelty, but because—it is the only warmth he can touch.

"Look." He whispers into the void, his voice like wind through dry leaves. "They are afraid."

And indeed, they are afraid. The lights in the carriage flicker. Passengers huddle together. Some pray, some weep, some use smiles to hide their inner trembling. And he stands in the crowd, wearing that cloth bag, quietly watching it all.

He can feel that fear slowly seeping into his body, making his power grow. 50 points, 100 points, 150 points... each increase in the number brings him one step closer to his more powerful form.

But he is not in a hurry.

Because he knows, fear needs time to brew. Too fast, and it's just a fright, a fleeting shock. Too slow, and it becomes numbness, being accustomed to despair. Only with the right rhythm, letting fear slowly ferment in the darkness, can the richest wine be produced.

So he learned to wait.

In corners, in the shadows, where no one is looking. He listens to heartbeats, watches the tremors, feels the mental defenses slowly crumbling. The process is beautiful, quiet, like a flower slowly blooming in the dead of night.

"Alright." Finally, one day, he says to himself. "It's time."

He walks towards the person with the strongest fear. The figure huddles in a corner, eyes glazed over, sanity having hit rock bottom.

Ma Chen Xu reaches out and gently touches the person's shoulder.

A second later, the figure collapses. No blood, no wound, just consciousness sinking into darkness.

Ma Chen Xu stands there, feeling the wave of fearful energy surge into him. His form is changing, growing stronger, more sinister, closer to the true nature of a ghost from ancient legends.

"More." He thinks. "A few more, and I can achieve my perfect form."

But at that moment, he sees another person.

A young man holding a strange talisman, his eyes showing not fear, but a peculiar determination.

"Clear Rain Talisman." The youth utters, the talisman burning, turning into a transparent barrier, blocking Ma Chen Xu's power.

Ma Chen Xu freezes for a moment.

This is the first time he has met someone like this. A person not afraid, or rather, someone who knows fear but chooses to fight it anyway.

"Interesting," he thinks.

He smiles. Even though no one can see his smile, the laugh indeed echoes in the darkness.

"Let's see then, how long you can hold out."

The train moves on, through darkness and light. Ma Chen Xu wanders in the shadows, his fear spreading, while the young man holding the talismans is getting closer and closer to his territory.

This game has only just begun.
star.story.role.guest_ghost:
She remembers being an ordinary person, living an ordinary life in some small city.
But that memory is very hazy, like looking at a landscape through a foggy window—the outlines clear, the details blurred.

She remembers boarding this train. No, not 'boarding'— being 'brought'. She woke up here, with a stack of talismans in her hand and strange knowledge in her head.

"You are the Guest Ghost," a voice said. "Your mission is to contain that ghost."

She didn't know which ghost, or why she was chosen. But she knew it wasn't an ordinary ghost. It was an ancient being that fed on fear, a monster that could shatter sanity in the darkness.

And she was the only hope against it.

She started learning to use the talismans.

The Clear Rain Talisman, could temporarily nullify the ghost's power. Prayer for Clear, could dispel darkness and restore sanity. Peachwood Nails, could put the ghost's skills on cooldown.

Each talisman required precise timing and accurate judgment. Use too early, waste it; too late, too late. She had to learn to observe, anticipate, and make the right choice in a split second.

It was not easy.

The ghost was powerful, could attack from the shadows, plunge everyone into maddening hallucinations. The Guest Ghost nearly failed several times, but each time, a strange instinct helped her dodge the fatal blow.

Later, she realized that wasn't instinct.

It was—connection.

There was an invisible link between her and that ghost. Perhaps fate, perhaps the rules, perhaps just the train's weird arrangement. She could 'feel' the ghost's presence, know where it was, what it was doing, even vaguely guess its next move.

The connection was strange, like an invisible thread binding them together.

"Why do you want to contain me?" the ghost once asked her, his voice coming from all directions without a specific source.

She thought for a moment, then said, "I don't know. Maybe because this train needs a balance."

"Balance?" The ghost laughed. "There's never been balance on this train. Only killing, only fear, only the law of the jungle."

"Then let's create it," she said.

The ghost fell silent.

Later, she found herself not only restraining the ghost. She also began protecting others. When the ghost's fear power spread, she would use talismans to weaken its effect. When someone's sanity was too low and nearing collapse, she would try to help them recover a little.

It wasn't her 'mission', but she felt it was what she should do.

Because she remembered being an ordinary person. She remembered the feeling of fear, the feeling of despair in the dark. She didn't want others to feel that way.

"You're becoming more and more... human," the ghost once said to her.

"I was always human," she replied.

"No, you're not anymore," the ghost said. "You are a Guest Ghost. You're part of this train, just like me."

She didn't speak.

Maybe the ghost was right. Maybe she was no longer an ordinary person, maybe her very existence was part of the train's bizarre rules. But she could still feel warmth, still feel compassion, still feel her still-beating heart.

That was enough.

As long as she could still feel, she would continue—containing that ghost, protecting others, maintaining a sliver of insignificant balance on this mad train.

"Ready?" she asked herself, gripping the talismans tightly.

"Ready."

Because that was her mission, whether she remembered who she used to be or not.
star.story.role.sea_king:
He stands underwater, surrounded by blue silence.

There is no wind, no waves, just the slow flow of water and the occasional distant school of fish. Sunlight filters through the ripples on the surface, forming pillars of light, like staircases to another world.

He is the Sea King, guardian of these waters.

He remembers being an ordinary person, living an ordinary life in some coastal city. But that memory is distant, like an old shipwreck on the seabed, rusty and corroded by the seawater.

Then, he came here.

Not brought, but summoned. These waters needed a guardian, someone to maintain order and expel intruders. So he stepped forward and took up that faithful trident.

The trident is the most powerful weapon in these waters.

Loyalty 3 means it will never betray its master. When thrown, if it hits its target, it kills instantly. And even if it misses, it returns automatically to its owner's hand.

But this power comes at a price.

The Sea King cannot use a revolver. That is the rule, the chain. He must rely solely on the trident, on his own strength.

"What are you guarding?" someone once asked.

"Guarding the peace of these waters," he answered. "Guarding what should not be disturbed."

But the arrival of this train broke everything.

The train traversed the seabed, bringing humans, bringing chaos, bringing danger. Those passengers moved through these waters, some good, some evil, and others—he couldn't tell.

He had to make a choice.

The trident glowed faintly in his hand, proof of its loyalty, symbol of his duty. He knew he had to use it, even if the cost was killing an innocent.

"I'll be careful," he told himself.

But being careful was never enough.

A few times, he threw the trident and hit someone he shouldn't have. Their bodies sunk to the seabed, slowly eroded by the water, until only bones remained.

Every accidental kill caused him a sharp pain. Not from injury, but from betraying his own beliefs.

"You are a sheriff, you must maintain order," a voice said. "But what is order? All good people alive, or all evil people dead?"

He couldn't answer.

Later, he understood that order was never absolute. In the chaos, all he could do was his best—protect the innocent, expel the intruders, restore peace to these waters.

Even if the cost was his own life.

"Ready?" he asked himself, gripping the trident.

"Ready."

Because that was his mission—guard these waters, guard what should not be disturbed, if only for a second of peace.
star.story.role.water_ghost:
He remembers being a drowning victim.

In that deep sea, he struggled, despaired, and was finally swallowed by the water. He remembered the feeling of suffocation, water filling his lungs, consciousness slowly fading, falling into eternal darkness.

But he didn't die.

Or rather, he died, but came back to life. But what came back was not his former self, but a new existence—a Water Ghost.

He could move freely in these waters, breathe like a fish, flow like water. The sea no longer threatened him; it was his home, his source of power.

In his hand, he held a trident, Riptide enchanted, allowing him to dash at incredible speed. It was his weapon, and his way of survival.

"You are a Killer," the voice said. "Your task is to eliminate all intruders."

He didn't want to kill. He remembered being an ordinary person, having a family, having friends, having a normal life. But those memories were fading, like old shipwrecks on the seabed, slowly eroding.

Now, he only knew one mission: eliminate.

So he began to act.

He lurked in the shadows, waiting for his moment. When an intruder came close, he would suddenly dash out, using the trident's extreme speed to instantly kill the target. Then disappear as quickly as he came.

The process was fast, clean, painless.

But he knew he was hurting the innocent.

Some intruders were just ordinary people, accidentally brought here by the train. They didn't know the danger of these waters, didn't know a killer was lurking here.

"You must return to the water," the voice warned him. "Otherwise, you will dry up and die."

This was his weakness. He couldn't stay on land for long; he had to return to the water regularly to rehydrate. This restricted his movements and made his survival harder.

"Why do you kill?" an intruder once asked him, in the last second before being killed.

He thought for a moment, then said: "Because I'm a Water Ghost."

"Don't Water Ghosts have hearts?"

He didn't answer.

Maybe they did, maybe they didn't. He couldn't tell if his heart still existed. He only knew that he had to kill, had to complete his mission, or he too would die.

Later, he noticed a strange phenomenon.

Every time he killed someone, his power would strengthen a little. Faster speed, stronger endurance, even allowed him to stay on land a little longer.

"That's the price of evolution," the voice said. "You must keep killing to maintain your existence."

He understood what that meant.

It was a path with no return. Once started, he couldn't stop. He had to keep killing until the last intruder fell, or until he himself dried up and died.

"Ready?" he asked himself, diving into the water.

"Ready."

Because that was his fate—a killer forever moving between water and land, a lonely soul forced to choose between killing and survival.
star.story.role.diver:
She remembers her first dive was when she was ten years old.

It was a sunny summer day. She was on vacation at the beach. Her father took her diving, and she saw the underwater world for the first time—colorful coral, schools of fish, and the peacefulness of being wrapped in water.

"How do you feel?" her father asked.

"Like another world," she replied.

From then on, she loved diving.

She got her diving license, bought her own gear, and went to the seashore whenever she could. She found herself especially relaxed and free in this blue world. As if she truly belonged here, not in the noisy world above.

Later, she became a professional diver.

She had explored countless seas around the world, dived many wrecks, and seen ruins forgotten by time. Each dive felt like an adventure, an exploration, a close encounter with the unknown.

Until this train appeared.

It passed through the seabed, her swept into the chaotic journey. She found her diving skills were unusually useful here—she could swim infinitely, she could breathe freely, she could serve as a guide for other passengers.

"You are a Civilian," a voice said. "Your task is to survive."

She didn't want to kill, nor be killed. She just wanted to survive in these waters and wait for the train's destination.

But she soon found it wasn't easy.

These waters were dangerous. Killers lurked. Water Ghosts roamed. There were various unknown threats. As a Civilian, she had to rely on her skills to find a way to survive.

She learned to observe.

Changes in the currents, behaviour of the fish, anomalies on the seabed—every detail could signal danger. She learned to react quickly, avoiding danger before it arrived.

She also learned to cooperate.

She met other Civilians, some who needed her help, others who could help her face dangers together. In these waters, it was hard to survive alone; only cooperation increased the chance of survival.

"Let me give you something," an ageing diver said to her one day. "This is my diving helmet, used for many years. It gives you underwater breathing and the dolphin's grace."

"What is the dolphin's grace?"

"It helps you to be faster and more flexible in the water." The aging diver smiled. "In these waters, speed is life."

She took the helmet and put it on. The feeling, like being protected, but also like being held.

"Thank you."

"You're welcome." The ageing diver patted her shoulder. "In these waters, we have to help each other."

Later, she found more equipment in the shop—diving boots, depth strider, all kinds of items to improve diving efficiency and survival ability.

She bought the equipment with her coins, gradually strengthening herself.

"Why do you need all that equipment?" someone asked.

"To survive," she replied. "And to help others survive."

"Why do you want to help others?"

She thought for a moment, then said: "Because it's what I can do. I'm a diver, my skills can be used to help others. If I don't help, it would be a waste."

The person laughed. "You're a really good person."

She didn't answer.

She didn't know if she was a good person. She only knew that in these chaotic waters, helping others was helping herself. Only if more people survived could this journey have any hope of reaching its destination.

"Ready?" she asked herself, putting on her diving helmet.

"Ready."

Because that was her mission—to survive in these waters and as far as she could, help others survive too. No matter how dangerous these waters were, no matter how chaotic this journey, she would never give up.

Because she was a diver, and this blue world was her home.
star.story.role.locksmith:
You were once a locksmith in this village. But ever since this train, strange things keep happening. Footsteps come and go outside, and you huddle in the corner, counting until daybreak.
Eventually, you formed a habit—check the door locks wherever you are. Reinforce them if you can, as many as you can. It's not fear of dying; it's just that you never want to hear that sound again—the sound of footsteps outside, knowing the door isn't thick enough.
star.story.modifier.tiny:
Fate, or some playful mutation, has left him a head shorter than most.
While this brings some inconveniences, it also allows him to move more nimbly through tight spaces and may make him easier to overlook.
star.story.modifier.tall:
Whether by genetic blessing or curse, he was born a full head taller than those around him.
This provides a better view but also makes him a more conspicuous target, often making him seem clumsy in low spaces.
star.story.modifier.feather:
He seems to have no mass, or rather, his existence itself seems to break physical rules.
He has no collision volume. He can flow easily through dense crowds, unblocked by others. But he cannot use his body to block or hold positions.
He moves between solids like a true ghost.
star.story.modifier.magnate:
Perhaps he has a natural gravitational pull for wealth, or perhaps he knows hidden ways to acquire it.
Every minute, small amounts of gold flow into his pockets automatically, like passive interest.
This slow but steady income gives him a slightly more comfortable economic position than others.
star.story.modifier.taskmaster:
A natural taskmaster, or perhaps a man obsessed with 'efficiency'.
He encourages labour and profits from all productivity.
star.story.modifier.allergist: "Have you ever eaten crayfish? Can you tell me what it tastes like? And what about mango?" His constitution deviates from the norm, reacting to food and drink with violent, unpredictable results. Every meal is a game of Russian roulette, full of surprises (or terrors).
star.story.modifier.cursed:
He is bound by an inescapable doom, a living carrier of a curse.
And when he dies, this curse does not dissipate; instead, it spreads like a contagious disease, passing to the one stained by his blood.
He is a sower of misfortune. Death is just the starting point of the curse's spread.
star.story.modifier.secretive:
He is a complete mystery, carrying his secrets into the grave.
Everything about him is completely locked away as his consciousness fades.
star.story.modifier.knight:
Darkness is his domain. Teleportation is his gift.
This uncontrollable ability could bring opportunity, or sudden, fatal crisis.
star.story.modifier.introverted:
He has been afraid of crowds since childhood. The noisy sounds, the scrutinising gazes—like countless needles pricking his skin, leaving nowhere to hide.

On the train, this feeling is magnified. Whenever more than two people are near, that suffocating fear climbs his back, and his sanity drains away like low tide.

But he found that when alone, or with just one other person, the pain dissipates, replaced by a strange peace. Sanity no longer drops, his mind becomes clear, as if the world has finally shown him a gentle side.

He learned to be alone. In corners, in abandoned carriages, in shadows where no one notices. There, it is safe, calm, the only place in this mad world where he can breathe.

"Why are you always alone?" asked someone.

He smiled and didn't answer. Because he knew that some people are simply not meant to survive in a crowd, just as some fish are not meant to breathe on land.
star.story.modifier.taxed:
He always felt that the rules of this world were harsher on him.

The bounty someone else got for a kill would be less in his hand. The automatic income others received would be reduced for him. 25%, not too much, not too little, like an invisible tax collector always by his side, taking its cut.

He protested, raged, even tried to reason with that invisible 'tax officer'. There was no response. Rules are rules, cold, unfeeling, unreasonable.

Gradually, he learned to accept it. Since he couldn't change it, he adapted. He would just kill more diligently, collect more greedily, using his increased effort to fill the invisible gap.

"The world is never fair," he thought, touching the pitifully few coins in his pocket, with a bitter smile. "But at least I'm still alive."

Being alive means there's still a chance. Even if the taxes never go away, even if the rules always lean against him—as long as he lives, one day, maybe he'll find a way to break this cage.
star.story.the_fool:
On the fog-shrouded train, there is a man, holding a set of incomplete tarot cards, calling himself "The Fool."

No one knows his real name, nor has anyone seen him without his mask. He always stands in the deepest part of the carriage, his gaze through the narrow gaps in his mask, watching every passing traveller.

"Do you believe in fate?" he might ask.

Most people will smile and shake their heads, continuing on their way. But some people stop, drawn by those deep eyes.

"Fate is not immutable," The Fool speaks softly, "But first, you must see its face."

Then he would hand out a piece of paper—golden, covered in symbols no one could read. He called this the 'Honored Note', the key to the realm above the mist.

Those who take the note and pray devoutly become members of the 'Tarot Club'. At The Fool's summon, their souls briefly leave their bodies, gathering above the mist. There, they vote, trying to identify those marked by fate—the 'Heretics'.

And the ancient pistol in The Fool's hand will only fire on the Heretic.

"I am not a judge," says The Fool, "I am merely the executor of fate."

"The journey is not yet over."
star.story.role.mortician:
He once worked as a night watchman at a funeral home, where he learned to deal with death.
The silent bodies, the cold metal, the delicate makeup—death wasn't an end for him, but a ceremony that needed to be performed with dignity.

The chaos on this train troubled him. People died without dignity, their bodies discarded like forgotten trash.

"At least," he thought, "I can send them off dignified."

His eyes were somewhat special, able to see scattered 'belongings' in the dark—items that had fallen from the dead, carrying their last memories.

His true skill was 'looting'—not greedy plundering, but a near-ritualistic act. He would approach the bodies, open their 'secondary inventory', and choose one or two valuable items from the deceased's belongings.

Was this profanity, or a form of respect? He couldn't say.

But one thing was certain: he couldn't touch those 'commands'—the power symbols woven by command blocks, controlling life and death. He wasn't here to become a new controller. He was just here to see them off.

Each body, he could only touch once. That was the rule, and his final respect for the dead.

"May you rest in peace," he would whisper, "and be safe."
